Settings

No matching settings
Appearance
Sans-serif (Inter) reads more naturally for prose; Monospace (Fira Code) is the original look, closer to a code editor. Applies everywhere you actually write — the outline, To-Dos, Pad, and Note — so it's consistent across the app rather than just the main editor. Tree-line connectors (│├└─) and code blocks always stay monospace regardless, since those are structure/code, not prose.
Off: only the Light/Dark switch above, changed by hand. System: follows your OS/browser's dark mode setting automatically. Schedule: switches at the hours you set below. In System or Schedule mode, clicking Light/Dark above still works — it holds as a temporary override until the schedule/system value naturally catches up and agrees with it again, then automatic switching resumes from there.
Dark starts at24-hour clock, 0–23
Light starts at24-hour clock, 0–23
100%
Background of the toolbar, sidebar, status bar, app bar, settings, help, and menus. Doesn't affect the editor/canvas writing surface.
Default
Bars
Status barShow the node count / depth / word count strip at the bottom. Also hideable via right-click on the status bar itself, with an Undo toast — this switch is the reliable way back if that toast is missed, since a hidden status bar has no space left to right-click on
Which individual items appear in the status bar strip, independent of the master Status bar switch above. The "⚠ Back up your data" reminder isn't included here — it only ever appears when your backup is genuinely overdue, so hiding it permanently would defeat the point; if it fires too often, raise the reminder interval in Settings → Data & backup instead.
Drag to reorder the right-hand cluster of the status bar (Saved status, backup indicators, theme, text size, author, and Quick Assist). Hidden items still keep their place in this list so the order is ready for when you turn them back on; the left-hand counters (node count, depth, word count) aren't reorderable — they stay in their fixed sequence.
Tab barShow the row of open-document tabs. Also hideable via right-click on empty tab bar space, with an Undo toast — this switch is the reliable way back if that toast is missed, for the same reason as Status bar above
App barShow the very top row (Documents/Templates, Settings, Help, Import/Export). Unlike Status bar and Tab bar above, this switch is not a reliable way back if you hide it and change your mind — Settings itself lives in this row, so once it's hidden you can't get back in here. Use Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+A, or right-click the editor canvas for "Show app bar," instead
Hide buttons you don't reach for often, to leave more room for Documents/Templates. Settings and Documents/Templates always stay — Settings needs to stay reachable, and Documents/Templates is core navigation.
Icon-only Import/ExportCollapse the Import and Export buttons down to just their icons, to save app bar space. Hover either one to see its name; clicking still opens the same dropdown.
Sidebar
New folders start collapsedApplies when you create a folder
Sidebar tipsBring back the dismissed right-click/drag hint
Filter boxTitle filter above the docs list (global search · Ctrl/Cmd+Space · covers docs, templates and node text)
Toolbar
Show toolbarQuick-access icons above the editor — also applies in zen mode; a floating button by the editor and Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+T mirror this switch
Group labelsCaption beneath each icon group
Word wrapWrap long lines in the editor. Turn off to scroll horizontally instead
ExpandedShow Extras as buttons instead of a menu
Customize toolbar buttonsAll buttons are shown
Hide any of the small buttons bottom-right of the editor (Zen, Pad, Toolbar, Preview) you don't use — the keyboard shortcut and the feature itself keep working either way, this only hides the button. For Pad, turning the whole feature off in Settings → Features hides their button too, regardless of this setting.
Pad
Pad PanelDocked panel for whole-document notes, Q&A, and Decision Log — separate from per-node notes. Toggle with the toolbar icon or Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+P. Which of its three tabs are available is set in Settings → Features → Pad Panel
Match editor backgroundOn: Pad's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
Default pad viewWhich tab greets you every time you open the Pad. You can still switch between tabs with the Pad's own header while it's open — this only sets the starting one.
Notepad
Hide buttons you don't use in the Pad's toolbar
Q&A
AI features for Q&ADraft answer, Bulk-draft unanswered, and Q&A summary live in Settings → AI, alongside every other AI-powered feature in the app — so everything using your API key and budget is visible in one place rather than split across each panel.
Compact Q&A rowsTighter spacing between separate Q&A rows, similar to the To-Dos panel's compact row spacing. Doesn't affect line spacing within a single question or answer — that stays comfortable to read either way.
Row actionsSame idea as the To-Dos panel's Row actions setting. "On hover" keeps rows compact — the select checkbox, drag handle, rewrite sparkles, link, and delete button collapse to zero width at rest so the row uses the full width, and expand only when you point at it. "Always visible" reserves that space permanently instead — less compact, but nothing moves or hides.
Decision Log
Ask who decidedPrompt for a name each time you insert a decision log — the prompt defaults to Default author if one is set. Off: skips the prompt and uses Default author automatically instead of leaving it blank. Timestamp is always added automatically either way
Color field labelsColor Context, Decision, Rationale, etc. so each field reads at a glance — in the editor and in the Decision Log card in Preview. Status is separate and always shows as a colored badge either way, since that's a status indicator rather than a field label.
Bold field labelsBold the field labels above, in addition to coloring them — in the editor and in Preview. Off keeps them the same weight as surrounding text — lighter, less shouty.
Split large ones onto their own slideIn Presenter and PPTX, a decision log with a lot of filled-in content gets a dedicated slide instead of being squeezed under the node's own bullets. Off: always inline, right after the node, however long it is.
Split thresholdCombined characters across the fields shown (see Decision card fields in Settings → Preview) before a decision log gets its own slide instead of staying inline
Pick which fields show in the Pad's decision card — turn off the ones you never fill in. Status is always shown separately as the cycling pill at the top, and the author/timestamp footer is always shown too
Note Panel
Backlinks@mention other nodes; existing [[mentions]] still render as links either way
Match editor backgroundOn: the note's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
Hide buttons you don't use in the per-node note panel's toolbar
Code
Match editor backgroundOn: the Code tab's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
To-Dos
AI features for To-DosExtract action items, Break into subtasks, Status summary, and its highlighting live in Settings → AI, alongside every other AI-powered feature in the app — so everything using your API key and budget is visible in one place rather than split across each panel.
Overdue count badgeShow a small count badge on the To-Dos button itself when you have overdue tasks, so you can tell at a glance without opening the panel.
Row actions"On hover" keeps rows compact and reveals drag/priority/due/rewrite/delete only when you point at a row. "Always visible" keeps them in place permanently — less compact, but nothing moves or hides.
Collapse sub-tasks by defaultNew sub-task checklists start collapsed instead of expanded, showing just the progress count until you click to open them.
Collapse completed section by defaultThe Completed list starts collapsed to just its header (with count) when you open the To-Dos panel, instead of showing every completed task. Click the header to expand it any time.
Row elementsTurn off any of these to remove them from every task row — useful if you only use a few of them and want a leaner list. Unchecking one only hides it; nothing already set on a task is lost, and it comes back the moment you check it again.
Auto-expire completed tasksAutomatically delete completed tasks after this long. "Never" keeps them until you delete them yourself.
Keep at mostOnce completed tasks exceed this count, the oldest completions are dropped first. "No limit" keeps everything until expiry or manual delete.
Meeting Notes
Collapse past meetings by defaultMeetings with a date before today move into a "Past meetings" section that starts collapsed to just its header (with count) when you open the panel, instead of showing every past meeting. Click the header to expand it any time.
Auto-expire past meetingsAutomatically delete past meetings after this long. Upcoming and undated meetings are never affected. "Never" keeps them until you delete them yourself.
Keep at mostOnce past meetings exceed this count, the oldest are dropped first. Upcoming and undated meetings don't count toward this limit. "No limit" keeps everything until expiry or manual delete.
Right-click menu
Customize right-click menuAll actions are shown
Zen mode
Hide toolbar by defaultAutomatically hide the toolbar when entering zen mode. If you show it again manually while zen, it stays shown until you exit
Hide Pad by defaultAutomatically close the Pad panel when entering zen mode, and restore it on exit if it was open before
Hide File Explorer (sidebar)Automatically hide the sidebar when entering zen mode. The header's sidebar toggle button still works normally in zen mode and always overrides this — if you show it again manually while zen, it stays shown until you exit
Hide status barHide the bottom status bar entirely while in zen mode. Off by default — zen mode instead trims the status bar down to just Saved status and the auto-rewrite indicator (plus Quick Assist, if it's normally docked in the app bar — see "Hide app bar" below), regardless of your normal status bar item selection, and restores your normal selection on exit
Hide tab barHide the row of open document tabs while in zen mode. Off by default — tabs stay visible so you can switch between open documents without leaving zen mode
Hide app barAlso hide the very top row (Documents/Templates, Settings, Help, Import/Export) while in zen mode — just the tree (and toolbar, if it's on) remains. On by default. If Quick Assist is normally docked in the app bar ("Dock in app bar" in Settings → Quick Assist), zen mode relocates it into the status bar for as long as zen mode is on, instead of peeking the app bar back open every time you invoke it — your actual "Dock in app bar" preference is untouched and Quick Assist returns to the app bar when you exit zen mode
Use browser fullscreenAlso request the browser's native fullscreen (like pressing F11) when entering zen mode — hides the browser's own tabs and address bar too, not just Sakura's UI. On by default. Exiting fullscreen (Esc, F11, or the browser's own control) exits zen mode too, to keep the two in sync
Presets
Editor's choice

A leaner layout for writing and presenting — trims the chrome around the outline and tunes Presenter mode for showing your work. Accent color and node text color are left untouched.

Toolbar, Sidebar, PadHidden
Hover toolbarOff
Tree linesHidden
Status barWords, Depth, Saved, Auto-rewrite only
App barVisible · Quick Assist docked here
Node Quick AssistIcon-only · Note, Code, Tags, Decision log, Rewrite
Editor backgroundsNote/Code/Pad don't match canvas
Toolbar buttonsCurated set, ready when shown again
Presenter modeLaser, branding, auto-start

Also runnable from Quick Assist ("apply editor's choice") — same effect as clicking Apply. Applying always leaves an Undo that restores every setting it touched, until you use it or apply again.

Documentation mode

Built for long-form structured documentation rather than presenting — full toolbar, full status bar, Decision Log and Tags front and center. The opposite instinct from Editor's Choice.

Toolbar, Sidebar, PadVisible, full button set
Hover toolbarOn
Tree linesVisible
Status barEverything visible
Node Quick AssistFull labels, every action
Decision Log, Tags, BacklinksOn · colored, bold labels
Editor backgroundsCode block stays distinct
Presenter modeOff

Also runnable from Quick Assist ("apply documentation mode"). Applying always leaves an Undo that restores every setting it touched, until you use it or apply again.

Editing
Outline numberingNumber sections 1, 1.1, 1.1.1
Confirm before deleteAsk before removing nodes. Note: if the node is referenced by backlinks, a warning always shows regardless of this setting
SpellcheckUnderline misspelled words while typing, using your browser's own dictionary. Right-click a misspelled word for suggestions — in Note and Pad, which have their own right-click menu, hold Shift while right-clicking to reach the browser's menu instead.
Date/time format
Default authorPre-fills the author on every new document, and any template's Date/Author line, so you don't have to set it each time. Existing documents are unaffected — change a single document's author from the status bar.
Node Quick Assist
Node Quick AssistWhile editing a node, Ctrl/Cmd+Space opens a small menu of fast node actions — insert em dash, date/time, note, code block, tags, rewrite — instead of the full Quick Assist. The same shortcut also opens a matching mini menu in the Pad, Note panel, Code block, and To-Dos task text — just the character-insert actions there (em dash, en dash, arrow, check mark, cross mark, middle dot, date/time), since note/code block/tags/rewrite only make sense pointed at a specific node. Off: Ctrl/Cmd+Space always opens the full Quick Assist everywhere, even mid-edit
Everything below configures what's in the mini menu once it's on. No typeahead — arrow keys + Enter, or click.
Icon-only rowShow the mini menu as a compact wrapping row of icons instead of a vertical list of icon+label rows. Hover an icon (or use arrow keys, same as before) to see its name. Same actions, same order — just a more compact layout.
Pick which fast actions appear in the mini menu while editing a node, and in what order — items always render in this fixed order regardless of which ones are on
Selection formatting popover
Show on text selectionA small Bold/Italic/Strikethrough/Highlight/Link/Rewrite row appears above selected text in the Note editor, the Pad's Notepad tab, and Meeting notes' Agenda/Notes fields — a quick way to format or AI-rewrite a highlighted phrase without reaching for the toolbar above. On by default.
Node hover toolbar
Hover ToolbarHover any node to reveal Menu and Zoom-in buttons next to it
Everything below configures what's in the Menu dropdown once it's on.
Pick which labeled actions appear in the dropdown when Menu is clicked. Zoom in on branch has its own dedicated button, so it isn't listed here. Defaults to the structural basics — add child, insert above/below.
Focus
FocusZoom into a branch — toolbar button, right-click action, and Ctrl/Cmd+.
Editor
Typewriter modeKeeps the line you're typing on pinned at a fixed height — the document scrolls under your cursor instead of your cursor drifting toward the bottom of the screen. Also keeps pace with arrow-key navigation. A manual scroll (wheel/trackpad) is left alone until you type or navigate again
Preview
Always start fully expandedOff starts every Preview fully folded to the top level instead
Which filled-in fields appear in a decision log's card in Preview, each as its own labeled block — kept short so it stays readable, but a field with children (e.g. a numbered Rationale or bulleted Impact list) still shows that nested content in full. Status always shows as a badge in the card header instead. Doesn't affect what's actually in the document, or the Decision Log feature itself
How headings get detected for Preview/PDF, and what shows up in the Contents panel.
Heading fallback by depth
Assign a heading level to a tree depth. In Preview, a node with no heading style of its own borrows the level assigned to its depth here. Depths left as "None" render as plain body text, exactly as they do today. Never overrides a heading you've actually applied in the editor.
Depth 0 (root)
Depth 1
Depth 2
Depth 3
Depth 4
Depth 5
Table of contents
Controls what shows up in the Contents panel and in the exported PDF's contents page.
Deepest level to includeHeadings below this level are left out of Contents, though they still render normally in the document. "All levels" includes everything.
Include section markersNodes written as [Section] act as top-level dividers in Preview. On by default; turn off to keep Contents to real headings only.
Treat depth 0 as section markersEvery top-level node acts as a [Section] divider automatically, without needing the [Bracket] text. Unlike "Include section markers" above, this changes the document itself — it affects the outline canvas (the same left-accent-border styling as a real [Section] node), Preview/PDF, and rich-text copy, not just what's listed in Contents. [Bracket] text still works at any depth, including depth 0, on top of this. Off by default, since turning it on changes how every existing top-level node looks.
Visual options for Preview only — none of these touch the editor or your document.
Reading width
1080px
Sepia reading themeWarm, paper-like background for Preview, independent of light/dark mode elsewhere in the app.
Serif body textBody text renders in a serif typeface for reading; headings stay sans-serif for contrast.
Options specific to the fullscreen ▶ Presenter mode in Preview.
Presenter slide breaks atWhich tree depth starts a new slide in Presenter mode. Depth 0 (default) means each top-level node is a slide; Depth 1 means each top-level node's direct children are, and so on.
Presenter zoomScales slide content while presenting — separate from the regular editor's text size. Defaults to 150%, since actual slide content (as opposed to a document you're reading up close) generally reads better larger from across a room.
150%
Use browser fullscreenAlso request the browser's native fullscreen when entering Presenter mode — hides the browser's own tabs and address bar too, not just Sakura's UI. On by default. Browsers show their own "press Esc to exit fullscreen" notice whenever this triggers — that's the browser's own security banner, outside any site's control, so turning this off is the only way to avoid it. With it off, Presenter mode still fills the browser window edge-to-edge; only the OS/browser chrome around it stays visible.
Auto-enable laser pointerTurn the laser pointer on automatically when entering Presenter mode, and off when you exit.
Open Preview straight into Presenter modeSkip the regular scrolling Preview entirely — opening Preview goes directly to the title slide, fullscreen. Esc then takes you all the way back to the editor in one press instead of dropping to the regular Preview first.
BrandingShows a small letter-spaced mark in the presenter bar while presenting, on the cover page of PDF and Word exports, and in the bottom-right corner of every page/slide of exported PDF, PowerPoint, and Word files. Reads "SAKURA" by default, or your own text below. Off by default — and off means off everywhere this applies, including the cover page.
Branding textReplaces "SAKURA" in the mark above with your own company or team name. Leave blank to use the default. Only shown when Branding (above) is on.
Elapsed timerA running clock in the presenter bar, from the moment you enter Presenter mode.
"All slides" gridThe grid button (and G shortcut) that shows every slide's label at once, for jumping around during Q&A.
Notes while presentingThe N shortcut that opens a floating notes panel without leaving Presenter mode — Q opens the same panel straight into its Q&A view. Drag its header to move it, drag the corner to resize it — double-click either to snap back to the default docked position — position and size are remembered next time. What you type is saved straight to this document's Pad (Settings → Editing → Pad) — the same notes are there afterward, nothing separate to manage. Requires Pad to be enabled.
Which of the Pad's toolbar buttons show in the floating Notes panel while presenting. This is independent from the Pad's own toolbar (Settings → Editing → Pad) — a smaller set here keeps the panel uncluttered mid-talk. Default: Bullet list and AI Rewrite only.
Layout
Compact rowsTighter spacing between separate rows, independent of text size. Doesn't affect line spacing within a single wrapped item — that stays comfortable to read either way.
Show note previews inlineA small italic snippet of a node's Note, truncated to fit, shown on the right side of that node — in the editor tree and in Preview/Presenter — so notes across many nodes can be scanned without clicking each note dot. Off by default.
Accent colour on branchesTint the tree branch connectors and lines with the active accent colour. Off by default — branches stay neutral.
Section markingsShows a coloured accent bar on section-header rows (nodes with a leading icon, like a book or document). On by default; turn off if it reads as visual noise alongside the tree branches.
Render section icons as SVGSwaps a recognised leading emoji on section-header rows for a crisp line icon matching the app's style. Only a curated set of emoji have a matching icon — turn this off if your document uses others too, so it doesn't end up a mix of SVG icons and plain emoji. View the icon library →
Fade nested textDeeper nodes fade toward grey as you nest, as a visual hierarchy cue (matches WorkFlowy/Dynalist/Roam). Off: every node uses the same Text color regardless of depth — raises contrast on deeply-nested text at the cost of the at-a-glance depth cue
Bold key labelsFor nodes written as "Label: value", bolds the label up to the colon so spec-style rows (Field: Something) scan like a spec sheet instead of a flat sentence. Only triggers on a plain word/phrase directly followed by a colon — doesn't affect Decision Log fields, links, or other node styling.
Hide tree linesClean indented view, no │├└─ connectors — like Dynalist/Workflowy. Toggle anytime with Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+L. Indentation and folding stay the same either way.
Depth guide linesOnly applies when Hide tree lines is on: shows a faint vertical line connecting sibling bullets, as a depth anchor once the branch connectors are gone. Off by default — fully clean view.
Timestamps on hoverHovering the tree connector or dot at the start of a node — not the text itself — shows when it was created and last modified. Turn off if the popup feels like clutter while you're just moving the cursor around.
Row selection styleHow selected rows are marked in the tree
Text size
100%
Limit reading widthCaps line length and centers the tree instead of spanning the full window — easier to read on wide monitors. Off by default. Preview has always had its own separate version of this.
Reading width
900px
Horizontal indent width per depth level — higher values give more visual separation between parent and child nodes
Startup
Start each session blankNew document each time, instead of reopening the last one
Reopen tabs on launchRestore whichever documents were open as tabs last time, instead of just one
Quick Assist
Quick Assist search boxShow the search / Quick Assist box, currently docked in the status bar (Ctrl/Cmd+Space opens and focuses it regardless; Quick Assist can also hide it via a command like "hide search bar" — this switch is the reliable way to bring it back)
Dock in app barMove the Quick Assist box up into the app bar (left of Settings) instead of the status bar. Off keeps it in the status bar strip; on docks it beside Settings at the top instead — its position there isn't reorderable the way the status bar cluster is. Ctrl/Cmd+Space keeps working the same either way, and zen mode's "Hide app bar" / "Hide status bar" switches follow wherever it currently lives.
Icon onlyCollapse the Quick Assist box down to just its icon when idle, to save status bar space. Click the icon, or use Ctrl/Cmd+Space as usual, to expand it to the full input — it collapses back down the same way it already closes (Escape, clicking elsewhere, or running a command). Has no effect if the search box above is hidden entirely, and only applies while Quick Assist is docked in the status bar — it's a no-op while "Dock in app bar" above is on.
Show search resultsBelow command matches, also show matching documents, notes, tags, and other content. Turn off to keep Quick Assist to commands only.
Show example phrasesThe "Try things like…" tip shown at the bottom of Quick Assist when the box is empty — click any phrase to run it as a search immediately, same as typing it. Turn off to hide the tip — Quick Assist itself keeps working the same either way.
Search results shownMaximum number of search hits, across all categories combined, shown below command matches
Pick which kinds of content and settings show up as "Go to" results in Quick Assist
Data
Backup all dataSaves every document, folder, and template to a single backup file — also includes AI provider keys and the GitHub Gist token (encrypted if Secure Storage below is on, plain text otherwise). Handle this file like you would a password: don't upload it anywhere you wouldn't put a key.
Restore from backupRestores from a backup file — replaces everything currently in the app
Auto-backup to fileWrites a live backup to a file on disk as you work. Chrome/Edge only — Safari and Firefox don't support this yet.
Up to 5 earlier snapshots of Auto-backup to file, at least 15 minutes apart — the live file always overwrites itself, so this is your way back to an earlier point if a bad edit gets backed up before you notice.
Local safety copyA second copy is kept automatically in a separate browser storage area, in case the main storage gets cleared. Not a substitute for Export.
Undo last restoreA snapshot of what was here right before your last Restore, in case that restore turns out to have been the wrong file.
Remind me to back upShows a status-bar warning if it's been longer than this since your last manual Export or auto-backup write.
Warn if this session isn't backed upStrict: the browser's own "leave site?" prompt on close attempt, then this dialog if you cancel it — a real last-chance gate, but you'll see the browser's generic popup. Passive: this dialog only, shown next time you switch away from or close the tab — no browser popup, but a direct close with no prior tab-switch gets no warning at all. Off: neither; the status-bar chip above is your only signal.
Why this mattersOpening a new version of this file starts with empty storage (browsers isolate it per file). Export before switching files, then Import right after to carry your data over. Auto-backup and the safety copy protect against accidental data loss, but only Export gives you a file outside the browser.
Reset to default settingsRestores appearance and behavior preferences (theme, toolbar, right-click menu, accent color, and the like) to their defaults. Doesn't touch your documents, folders, templates, or AI provider settings.
Restore built-in templatesRecreates any built-in template — or the Built-in folder — you deleted or renamed, using the original shipped content. Templates you duplicated or made yourself aren't touched.
Secure Storage
Encrypt keys at restEncrypts your AI provider keys and the GitHub Gist token — in this browser's storage and in local backup files — with a passphrase only you know. Off by default; keys are stored as plain text until you set one. There's no recovery if you forget the passphrase.
Not set up — keys are stored as plain text.
Cloud Backup
What this doesPushes the same full backup as "Backup all data" above to a cloud account of your choice, so you can pull it back down from a different browser or computer. Entirely optional — local Backup/Restore always works without connecting either of these.
Backs up to a secret Gist on your GitHub account. Needs a Personal Access Token with the gist scope — create one here. Stored in this browser only, the same way your AI provider keys are.
Not connected.
Auto-sync on editPushes to Gist automatically ~1.2s after edits settle, same timing as Auto-backup to file. If the Gist changed elsewhere since your last Save/Load here (e.g. from another browser), auto-push pauses and flags it below rather than overwriting — resolve with a manual Save or Load.
Backs up to a file Sakura creates in your Google Drive — scoped so the app can only see files it created itself, not the rest of your Drive. Needs a free OAuth Client ID from Google Cloud Console, with added as an authorized JavaScript origin.
Not connected.
Auto-sync on editPushes to Drive automatically ~1.2s after edits settle, same timing as Auto-backup to file and Gist auto-sync. Only active while connected this session — Drive's sign-in token isn't kept across reloads, so this turns itself off (with a one-time notice, not a nag) whenever the session connection is gone, and you'll need to reconnect and re-enable it next time.
Trash
Keep deleted documents & templates forDeleted documents and templates both go to the same Trash, not gone for good. After this long they're purged automatically — pick "Forever" to only ever remove them by emptying Trash yourself.
Include Trash in searchQuick Assist's search results (Ctrl/Cmd+Space) normally only look at active documents and templates. Turn this on to also surface matches sitting in Trash, clearly marked as such.
Empty TrashPermanently deletes every document and template currently in Trash. This cannot be undone.
Export & print
Skip folded sectionsOff: exports and printing always include everything. On: only what's currently expanded is included. Applies to Markdown, OPML, Text, and the header Print button — not PDF, PowerPoint, or Word, since those render from (or mirror) Preview/Presenter, which already has its own fold state under your direct control there (click any section to expand or collapse it) and is left exactly as you set it.
Include Q&A in exportsAppends the Pad's Q&A list as a final section in Word and PDF exports — bold question, regular-weight answer, unanswered rows marked "No answer provided". Off: Q&A stays in the Pad only, out of exported files.
Include Notepad in exportsAppends the Pad's whole-document Notepad content as a final section in Word and PDF exports. Off: Notepad stays in the Pad only, out of exported files.
Include Decision Log in exportsIncludes each node's decision log card in Word, PDF, and PowerPoint exports. Off: decision logs stay visible in the app only, out of exported files — separate from the Decision Log feature toggle itself.
Include node Notes & Code in exportsIncludes each node's attached Note and Code block in Word, PDF, and PowerPoint exports — every note/code card is included regardless of what's expanded on screen in Preview. (PowerPoint currently only pulls diagram images out of a note, not the note or code text itself.) Also controls whether OPML's per-node _note attribute is written. Off: excluded from all of the above entirely, including the small "has a note/code" indicator dots — nothing in the PDF hints that a hidden note exists. Preview itself is unaffected; this only changes what an exported file actually contains.
Include Diagrams in exportsIncludes each node's linked draw.io diagram (Pad → Diagrams tab) in Word and PowerPoint exports, same as it already appears in Preview and PDF. Off: diagrams stay visible in the app and PDF only, out of Word/PowerPoint files.
Folded-content notice in Export menuThe reminder that appears in the Export ▾ menu whenever anything is folded ("Skips folded sections… Include them" or the reverse) — same info as the setting above, just surfaced right where you're about to export. Turn off once you know the behavior and don't need the reminder each time; the underlying Skip folded sections setting itself is unaffected.
Cover pageAdd a title page (document title, author, word/read-time/decision stats) before the contents page when exporting to PDF or Word. The small wordmark above the title only appears if Settings → Preview → Presenter mode → "Branding" is also on.
PDF footerAdds a running footer to every PDF page: export date (bottom-left), page numbers (bottom-center). Renders as real CSS page-margin content, not an overlay, so it can't end up overlapping the page's own content — Chrome-based browsers only; unsupported browsers just show no footer.
PDF page marginsDefault margin size when exporting to PDF — the print dialog that opens still lets you override this per export.
Hide items you don't use from the header's Export ▾ menu. Default: Copy as Image, Word, PDF, and PowerPoint — the rest are hidden but a click away here.
Debug Log
Enable debug loggingKeeps a rolling log (last 500 entries, in memory only) of save, restore, and panel events — meant for tracking down exactly what happened around an issue like data not saving. Never includes your actual note/task/document text, only metadata like node IDs, lengths, and which action ran.

            

          

          
          

            
Provider

            

              

                Provider
                
▾

              

              

                Custom providers
                
              

              

                

                All six built-in providers can’t be edited or removed. Every provider needs an API key — select one from the Provider dropdown above to see where to get it. GitHub Models requires a fine-grained personal access token with models:read scope. Add any additional OpenAI-compatible, Gemini-style, or Anthropic-style API with “+ Add provider” — user-added providers can be edited or deleted.
              

              

                Model
                
▾

              

              

                
              

              

                API Key
                

                  
                  
                  
                  
                

                No key saved.
                
                No requests to this provider yet today.
              

              

                
Fallback orderWhen any error occurs on the active provider (quota, rate limit, or other failure), Sakura retries with the next checked provider in order. The active provider is always tried first. Providers without a saved key are skipped. A toast confirms which provider was used.

                
              

              

              

              
Reset AI settingsRestores provider, model, and rewrite prompt to their defaults, and clears any saved API key. Separate from the general settings reset — your documents and other app preferences aren't touched either way.

            

          

          

            
Rewrite

            

              
Rewrite max response lengthCaps output tokens for the Rewrite feature specifically (editor and Pad). Generate Outline (2048), Restructure Text (4096), and short tasks like Summarise/Tags already use their own larger or smaller caps tuned to what they do, and aren't affected by this. Raise this if rewriting a long selection gets cut off.

              

                Rewrite prompt
                
                

                  
                  
                  
                

              

            

          

          

            
Auto-rewrite on commit

            

              

                
Auto-rewrite on commitRuns Rewrite automatically on nodes as you finish editing them — no manual click. Queued and sent as a single batched request after a short pause or once enough nodes have queued up (both configurable below), not one request per node, to avoid burning through provider rate limits during continuous note-taking. Uses the same Rewrite prompt configured above — there's no separate prompt for auto-rewrite. Requires an API key configured above; if one isn't available right now (none set, or Secure Storage is locked), queued nodes wait rather than being discarded and send automatically as soon as a key becomes usable again. Turns itself off after repeated AI-call failures rather than retrying silently forever — a separate case from simply not having a key yet. Shows a live status in the bottom status bar whenever it's on.

                
              

              

                Auto-rewrite exclusions — text matching any checked category is left alone, since "form a sentence" doesn't apply to it and rewriting it risks corrupting syntax Sakura parses specially. Unchecking a box lets auto-rewrite touch that category too.
                
                
                
                
                

                  Minimum word count
                  
                

                Nodes shorter than this are treated as labels, not sentences-in-progress, and skipped. Range 0–20; 0 disables the length check entirely.
                

                  Idle pause before sending
                  
                

                Seconds of no further edits before the queued nodes are sent as one batch. Lower sends sooner but in smaller batches; higher lets more nodes accumulate per request. Range 1–300 seconds.
                

                  Nodes queued before sending
                  
                

                If this many nodes queue up before the idle pause elapses, they're sent immediately rather than waiting — keeps a long capture session from growing the batch past what one request can handle well. Range 2–50.
                

                  Show queued count in chip
                  
                

                The status bar chip shows "N queued" while a batch is pending. Off: just shows that something's queued, without the number.
                

                  Show countdown in chip
                  
                

                The chip shows a live "sends in Xs" countdown to the next batch, resetting every time another node joins the queue. Off if you find the ticking distracting rather than useful.
              

            

          

          

            
To-Dos AI

            

              
Extract action itemsAdds a button in the To-Dos panel header that reads the current document (or your selected nodes, if any are selected) and proposes a batch of tasks from it, added directly with a single Undo. Uses the same provider/key as Rewrite above, and needs AI Capabilities on.

              
Break into subtasksAdds an AI button on each task, next to Rewrite, that proposes 3-6 concrete subtasks and adds them directly, with Undo.

              
Status summaryAdds a button in the To-Dos panel header that writes a short plain-language summary of your open tasks — what's overdue or urgent first, then general progress — shown in a dialog with a Copy button.

              
Highlight key details in summaryBolds task names and colors overdue mentions, due dates, priority, and status words in the Status summary dialog, using the same colors as the To-Dos row chips — so you can scan it instead of reading every sentence. Off shows plain text.

            

          

          

            
Q&A AI

            

              
Draft answerWhen a Q&A row's answer is empty, its Rewrite sparkle drafts one instead of doing nothing — using the question plus any outline content nested under its source node as context. The same button keeps rewriting non-empty answers either way; this only covers the empty case.

              
Generate questionsAdds a button in the Q&A toolbar that reads the document and suggests relevant questions worth asking about it — not just nodes that already end in "?" (that's the separate Scan button). Uses whatever's selected in the outline as its scope (a single node's whole section, or exactly the nodes in a multi-select) — with nothing selected, it reads the whole document. Opens the same review checklist as Scan, so nothing is added until you confirm.

              
Group into sectionsAdds a button in the Q&A toolbar that reads every question in the list and organizes them into a small number of named sections by topic, using the section-header rows already available from the + menu. Replaces any existing section groupings with a fresh one built from scratch, rather than layering on top of them — every question keeps its answer, follow-up flag, and Answered by; only the ordering and section headers change. Undo restores the previous arrangement in one step.

              
Bulk-draft unansweredAdds a button in the Q&A toolbar that runs AI drafting over every unanswered row in one pass, not just the ones you've selected.

              
Q&A summaryAdds a button in the Q&A toolbar that writes a short summary of what's answered and what's still open, shown in a dialog with a Copy button.

            

          

          

            
Features

            
Master on/off switches for the bigger, optional capabilities. Turning one off hides every entry point for it — toolbar buttons, right-click menu items, and the matching Settings and Help sections.

            

              
Node content

              

                
Note PanelPer-node notes and code attachments — toolbar button, right-click action, and the panel itself

                
Code tabThe Note panel's Code tab — syntax-highlighted per-node code attachment, opened from the same Note entry point as prose

                
Tagging#tags on nodes — insert button, right-click action, and the tag manager

              

            

            

              
Navigation & workspace

              

                
Pad PanelDocked whole-document notepad, separate from per-node notes

                
NotepadThe Pad's free-form notes tab

                
Q&AThe Pad's structured question/answer tab

                
Decision LogThe Pad's structured decision-entry tab — insert button, right-click action, and Excel export. At least one of these three tabs must stay on

                
DiagramsThe Pad's draw.io diagram tab — create diagrams and link them to nodes so they appear in Preview and PDF exports

              

              

                
To-DosApp-level task list, separate from documents — its dock button (bottom-right of the screen) and the To-Dos panel itself

                
Meeting NotesApp-level meeting log, separate from documents — its dock button (bottom-right of the screen) and the Meeting Notes panel itself

                
Quick AssistCtrl/Cmd+Space command box — type plain-English instructions like "hide sidebar" and they run immediately, run one-off actions like "new document" or "duplicate node," run AI actions like "rewrite document" or "expand node" (each reuses the same AI feature as its toolbar button, so it needs an API key configured under AI Rewrite and respects AI Capabilities being on), or type anything else to search documents, notes, tags, settings, and help. Toggles, one-off actions, and settings changes are instant and reversible with Undo; AI actions are the exception — they call the configured AI provider and can take a few seconds or fail like any other AI call, but their results are still added to Undo once they land

              

            

            

              
Automation

              

                
AI CapabilitiesRewrite, expand, summarise, generate outline, suggest tags/icon, and Restructure text (Import menu) — the AI toolbar group and matching right-click/import items. Word import's automatic fallback for documents with no heading structure also needs this on

              

            

            

              

                

                  
                  Full
                

                

                  
                

              

              

                
Turns off every optional feature above in one click, all at once — a stripped-down outliner with just the tree, nothing else competing for attention. This is the same on/off state as toggling each row above individually; it's a shortcut for all eleven, not a separate mode.

                

                  
Note Panel & Code tabOff

                  
TaggingOff

                  
Pad PanelOff · Notepad, Q&A, and Decision Log tabs go with it

                  
Decision LogOff

                  
To-Dos & Quick AssistOff

                  
AI CapabilitiesOff

                  
Focus modeUnaffected — it's core navigation, not an optional extra

                

                
No separate Undo here — Restore Full flips every one of the above back on in one click. If you'd only turned a few of them off by hand before going minimal, Restore Full turns all eleven back on rather than reproducing that exact partial state, so re-toggle any you want off again afterward.

              

            

          

          

            
Keyboard Shortcuts

            

              
Customize shortcutsAll shortcuts are at their defaults

            

          

          
Everything is stored locally in your browser by default — nothing leaves this tab unless you turn on an opt-in feature. AI actions (Rewrite, Expand, etc.) send that node's content to whichever AI provider you've configured, and Cloud Backup (Gist/Drive) uploads a copy to that service, but only while connected and only when you use them.

            

          

        

      

      

        
        

          

            
Help

            

              
              
              
            

            
          

          

            

              
              
              
              
              
              
              
              
            

            

          
No matching help topics

          

          
New here? Take the guided tour.

          

          
Quick reference for everything below.

          
Curious why an outliner helps?

          
See it all come together, end to end.

          

          
Pick a category on the left for reference on any part of Sakura — shortcuts, panels, AI, and everything else.

          

          
  

Core editing
  

      

  • Enter / F2 → edit
    •   

  • Enter → new sibling below (while editing)
    •   

  • Shift + Enter → split at the cursor — text after the cursor moves into a new sibling below, text before it stays put
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Enter → new child — works whether you're editing a node or just have one selected
    •   

  • Type → start editing
    •   

  


          
  

Navigation
  

      

  • Click → select
    •   

  • Shift + Click → range
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Click → multi-select
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + A → select all
    •   

  


          
  

Quick Assist
  

      

  • Lives in the header, where a plain search box used to sit. Ctrl/Cmd + Space focuses it and opens the results dropdown from anywhere in the app; clicking directly into the box does the same. Type a plain-English instruction and it's carried out immediately: "get me toolbar," "I don't want sidebar," "get rid of pad," "toggle dark mode" — or just type anything else to search, the same as the old separate Global Search box (formerly Ctrl/Cmd+K, now folded into this one shortcut and box) did
    • 

  • An empty box shows a "Try things like…" tip with a handful of example phrases — click any of them to run it as a search immediately, same as typing it yourself; nothing executes without a second click on the actual result. One of the examples, "apply editor's choice," is the fastest way to reach the preset covered below without needing to open Settings at all. Turn the tip off in Settings → Workspace → Quick Assist if you'd rather not see it — the box itself works identically either way
    • 

  • To search just one category instead of all eleven at once, type a category prefix before your query — "notes: budget" only searches Notes, "settings: dark" only searches Settings. Short aliases work too (doc/docs, note/notes, tag/tags, feature/features, and so on). Don't want to remember the exact word? Click the icon at the left of the box, or press Space while it's completely empty — either shows a picker inline in the dropdown, navigable the same way search results are (↓ / ↑ to move, Enter to pick). Above the eleven categories, the same picker also lists Show, Hide, Toggle, and Run — picking one inserts that bare word instead of a category prefix, which does something different: rather than scoping a search, it browses everything of that kind (see the bare-verb bullet below). Either way, choosing an entry leaves the dropdown open, cursor ready for you to keep typing and narrow further. A category prefix with nothing typed after it (just "notes: " on its own) shows no results rather than browsing the whole category — the box doesn't have a "show me everything in here" mode for categories, only "search within here," so you still need something to search for after a category prefix specifically; the four verb words above are the exception; each of those does browse everything on its own with nothing else typed
    • 

  • Typing just a bare word — "show," "hide," "toggle," or "run," nothing else — lists everything of that kind instead of requiring you to already know a specific setting's name: "show" lists everything currently hidden that could be shown, "hide" lists everything currently shown that could be hidden, "toggle" lists everything either way regardless of its current state, and "run" lists every available Run action ("new document," "duplicate node," "insert decision log," "editor's choice preset," "documentation mode preset," and the AI actions covered below). Keep typing after the bare word to narrow the list further by name — "show tool" narrows to just toolbar-related results, the same as typing "show toolbar" would, just built up in two steps instead of one
    • 

  • On a Mac, if the system's own Spotlight is bound to Cmd+Space (its default), it takes priority over the browser and this shortcut won't fire — either change Spotlight's shortcut in macOS System Settings → Keyboard, or remap this one from Settings → Keyboard Shortcuts
    • 

  • Toggles and search are rule-based, not AI — a fixed set of recognized phrases (show/get/enable/turn on… vs. hide/remove/get rid of/turn off…) matched against a fixed list of settings. Nothing is sent anywhere for these, no API key needed, and it never improvises — if it doesn't recognize a phrase, it says so rather than guessing. The AI Run actions below are the one exception: those do call your configured AI provider, the same as clicking their toolbar/right-click equivalent would
    • 

  • Covers the everyday visibility toggles: toolbar (and its group labels), sidebar (and its filter box), Pad, zen mode, dark/light mode, auto-rewrite on commit, tree lines, timestamps on hover, spellcheck, backlinks, compact rows, word wrap, outline numbering, the Quick Assist search box itself and its icon-only mode, icon-only Import/Export in the app bar, the hover toolbar, delete confirmation, decision log colors, accent-colored branches, expanded toolbar, and the startup tab/blank-session preferences — plus every on/off switch for a whole capability, whether it lives in Settings → Features (Note Panel, Code Block, Tagging, AI Capabilities, Pad Panel, To-Dos, Quick Assist) or inside that panel's own section (Decision Log, Focus, Backlinks, Hover Toolbar, Node Quick Assist). It does not cover destructive actions (reset, delete, empty trash) or multi-value settings (themes, sliders) — those still live in Settings, on purpose
    • 

  • Note Panel, Code Block, Tagging, and Decision Log are reachable with slightly longer phrases than you might expect — "note panel feature," "code block feature," "tags feature," "decision log feature" — rather than their bare names. That's deliberate: those bare words are also what Quick Assist searches for as content (or, for Decision Log, what every inserted entry starts with), so a short keyword there would rank a toggle above the search hit you were actually looking for. Pad Panel is qualified too ("pad feature") for a different reason — bare "pad" is already claimed by the command that opens or closes the Pad panel for this session, which is a separate thing from turning the whole feature on or off
    • 

  • Below command matches, a separate row type labeled Run covers one-off actions instead of toggles: "new document," "duplicate node," "duplicate," "editor's choice preset" (or "apply editor's choice"), and "documentation mode preset" (or "apply documentation mode") — or type the bare word "run" with nothing else to list all of them at once, the same way a bare "show" or "hide" lists every matching toggle. These don't have an on/off state to show or an existing value to report — they just do the thing once, the same as clicking the equivalent toolbar or Settings button would. Only safe, additive, undoable operations are ever reachable this way; nothing destructive is offered as a Run action, same rule as the toggles above. "Duplicate node" needs a node selected first — if nothing's selected, it still shows up (so you know it exists) but greyed out with "select a node first" instead of a working button, rather than either hiding it or letting a click do nothing unexplained. Undo means different things per action: for "new document" and "duplicate node" it's the regular document Undo; "editor's choice preset" instead restores every setting it touched (toolbar, sidebar, Pad, hover toolbar, accent, text color, right-click menu) back to what they were immediately before, since none of those live on the document undo stack
    • 

  • AI Run actions — also listed under "run," these are the exception to "rule-based, not AI" above: "rewrite document" (the whole open document, same as right-click → Rewrite document), "rewrite this node" / "rewrite selected" (current selection, single or multiple nodes), "expand node" / "expand into subtree" (needs exactly one node selected), "summarise selection" (needs 2 or more nodes selected), "suggest tags" (needs exactly one node selected), "generate outline" (opens the same topic-prompt dialog as its toolbar button), and "restructure text" (opens the same paste-text dialog). Each needs an API key configured in Settings → AI → Provider and disappears entirely if AI Capabilities (Settings → Features) is off — same requirement as the toolbar buttons they reuse. Unlike the rule-based actions above, these take a moment to run and can fail (expired key, provider rate limit) exactly like clicking their toolbar equivalent would — a missing key or a failed call surfaces the same error dialog either way, not a silent no-op. Undo works the regular way once a result lands, since each reuses the exact same function — and therefore the exact same pushUndo() call — as its toolbar/right-click counterpart
    • 

  • Below any command and action matches, the same box also surfaces matching documents, node text, notes, tags, the pad, and settings/help topics — covering settings, help, document and template names, node text across every document (open or not), per-node Notes, per-node Code blocks, tags, and each document's Pad. These rows are labeled Go to instead of Show/Hide, since picking one navigates you there rather than changing anything, and each shows a short snippet of where the match sits so you're not guessing what you're about to open
    • 

  • Clicking a "Go to" result does the right thing for its type: a node-text match jumps to that node and highlights the match; a Notes match opens that node's Note panel; a Tags match jumps to the tagged node; a Pad match opens that document's Pad
    • 

  • Skips Trash by default. Settings → Trash → "Include Trash in search" surfaces matches there too, across every content type above, clearly marked "In Trash"; clicking one reveals it in the Trash section rather than opening it directly, since restoring is a deliberate choice
    • 

  • Rich-text fields (Notes, Pad) are stripped to plain text before matching, so a search never hits raw HTML markup and snippets always show readable text
    • 

  • The sidebar's own filter box only matches document/template names, not their contents — for anything inside a document, this box is the one that actually looks
    • 

  • Every document has a status chip — No status by default, or Draft, Review, Approved, Rejected — next to the title field. Click it to open a small menu and pick one; it's a menu rather than a cycle-through-in-order control like the diagram/decision-log status chips, since jumping straight to the one you want beats clicking past the others first. Existing documents start with no status until you set one; it's never assumed. Duplicating a document resets its copy to No status rather than carrying the original's over, same reasoning as why the title gets "(copy)" appended instead of reused verbatim — a fresh copy hasn't itself been through review or approval. Version History restores status alongside everything else for versions saved after this feature shipped; older versions predating it simply leave the current status untouched on restore rather than guessing
    • 

  • Settings → Workspace → Quick Assist controls the search side of this specifically: a switch to turn search results off entirely (command-only), how many results show at once, and which categories (Documents, Notes, Tags, Settings, Help, and so on) are eligible to appear at all
    • 

  • As you type, the list runs in a fixed order top to bottom — matching commands first, then Run actions, then search results — each labeled with what it'll do ("Hide Sidebar," "Show Toolbar," "Run: New document," "Go to: …") and, for toggles, the setting's current state; ↓ / ↑ moves through the combined list (disabled Run rows are skipped, since there's nothing to activate), Enter runs or opens whichever is highlighted, Esc closes and clears the box, or click any of them directly
    • 

  • Every command and Run action shows a toast with an Undo button — nothing is ever silently applied. Just typing a bare toggle name with no verb ("toolbar," "sidebar") flips its current state rather than doing nothing. If a Run action has its own confirmation step (Insert decision log asks who's deciding first) and you cancel that step, nothing happens and the toast says so instead of falsely claiming success. "Go to" rows are navigation, not a change to anything, so there's nothing to undo for those
    • 

  • If a phrase matches more than one setting, all of them show up as separate ranked options instead of Quick Assist silently picking one for you
    • 

  


          


Node Quick Assist


    

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Space while actively editing a node opens a small menu of node-specific actions instead of the full Quick Assist — same shortcut, different box, depending on whether you're mid-edit. Everywhere else, it opens the full Quick Assist as usual
    • 

  • Default actions, in order: Insert em dash, Insert en dash, Insert arrow, Insert check mark, Insert cross mark, Insert middle dot, Insert date/time, Note…, Tags…, Rewrite node, Version history…. Which of these appear (and their fixed order) is configured in Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist → Menu items
    • 

  • Version history… — also available by right-clicking a node (in the "notes" group of the More panel, or promotable to the quick row via Settings → Right-click menu → Menu items) — shows earlier text for just this one node, drawn from the document's regular version history (captured automatically roughly every 10 minutes of active changes — see the Version History entry below) — skips straight past versions where this particular node didn't change, so a document with 20 saved versions might show far fewer than 20 rows here. "Restore text" replaces only this node's text, through the normal undo stack (Ctrl/Cmd+Z undoes it like any other edit) — nothing else about the node (note, code, tags, decision log) is touched
    • 

  • No typeahead — unlike @mentions or the full Quick Assist, this menu doesn't filter as you type. ↓ / ↑ moves through it and wraps at either end, Enter runs the highlighted action, Esc or resuming normal typing dismisses it. There's deliberately nowhere for a typed query to live except the node's own text, which this avoids touching
    • 

  • Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist → Icon-only row switches the menu from a vertical icon+label list to a compact wrapping row of icons — same actions, same order, same keyboard navigation, just a smaller footprint. Hover an icon for its name
    • 

  • Insert em dash, Insert en dash, Insert arrow, Insert check mark, Insert cross mark, and Insert date/time all splice directly at the cursor and keep you in edit mode, so you don't lose your place — the fastest actions in the menu, and the reason it exists. Note, Code block, Tags, and Insert decision log commit the edit first and open their normal panel, exactly as right-click → the same item would. Rewrite node runs the same AI rewrite as the toolbar/right-click version, including its own "Rewritten ✦" confirmation toast
    • 

  • The popup and any panel it opens (Tags, Note, Code block) position themselves off the actual cursor location, not just the node row — accurate even on a long, wrapped node where the cursor isn't near the top of the row
    • 

  • Note, Code block, Tags, Decision log, and Rewrite node each drop out of the menu automatically if their own feature is turned off (Settings → Features), same as everywhere else those actions appear
    • 

  • Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist turns the whole thing off — Ctrl/Cmd+Space then always opens the full Quick Assist, even mid-edit, same as before this feature existed
    • 




          
  

Appearance & Display
  

      

  • Theme & accent — Settings → Appearance: Light/Dark theme, seven accent colours, and an intensity slider. Auto theme sits below it — Off (the Light/Dark switch only, changed by hand), System (follows your OS/browser's dark mode setting live, no polling needed), or Schedule (switches at hours you set, checked once a minute plus whenever the tab regains focus, to catch changes that happened while it was backgrounded or the machine was asleep). Manually clicking Light/Dark in System or Schedule mode holds as a temporary override until the schedule/system value naturally catches up and agrees with it again — e.g. forcing dark during a scheduled light window holds through the next scheduled dark transition and then continues following the schedule normally from there, rather than reverting within the next check. "Accent colour on branches" (Settings → Layout) tints the tree connector lines with the accent too; off by default so branches stay neutral
    • 

  • Content font (Settings → Appearance, below Theme) — Sans-serif (Inter) or Monospace (Fira Code) for the text you actually write: the outline, To-Dos, Pad, and Note all switch together, so it's one consistent choice rather than a per-panel setting. Tree-line connectors (│├└─), code blocks, and inline code always stay monospace regardless of which is picked — those are structure/code, not prose, and switching them would hurt rather than help. Sans-serif is the default; Monospace is the original look for anyone who prefers it
    • 

  • Chrome background (Settings → Appearance, below Accent) — five presets: Default (follows the Light/Dark theme), Slate, Sand, Ink, and Rose. Recolors the toolbar, sidebar, status bar, app bar, settings, help, and menus; the editor/canvas writing surface is untouched, so your outline keeps reading against its own background regardless of which preset is picked. Independent of both the accent color and the Light/Dark theme — any combination of the three is valid
    • 

  • Text color (Settings → Appearance, below Chrome background) — four presets for node text specifically: Default, Black, Charcoal, and Slate. Separate from the accent color (which colors buttons, borders, and highlights, not node text) and from Chrome background (which colors app chrome, not the outline itself)
    • 

  • Status bar items (Settings → Bars & Menus, under the Status bar switch) — which individual pieces show in the bottom strip: node count, max depth, word count, current depth, current node's word count, saved status, backup connection status, the auto-rewrite indicator, theme chip, text size control, and author field, each independently hideable. The master Status bar switch above still controls the whole strip; this is finer-grained, for keeping only what you actually use. The "⚠ Back up your data" reminder chip alone isn't in this list on purpose — it's an actionable alert that only appears when your backup is genuinely overdue, and permanently hiding an alert defeats it; if it fires too often, raise the reminder interval in Settings → Data & backup instead
    • 

  • Status bar item order (Settings → Bars & Menus, right below Status bar items) — drag to reorder the right-hand cluster: saved status, backup connection status, the backup-overdue reminder, the auto-rewrite indicator, theme chip, text size control, author field, and Quick Assist. Hidden items keep their place in this list, so turning one back on doesn't reset where it sits. The left-hand counters (node count, depth, word count) are a separate, fixed-order cluster and aren't part of this — only the right side is reorderable
    • 

  • App bar items (Settings → Bars & Menus, under the App bar switch) — hide Help, Print, Import, and Export individually if you rarely reach for them, to leave more room for Documents/Templates. Settings and Documents/Templates always stay in the app bar — Settings needs to stay reachable (same reasoning as the App bar master switch above), and Documents/Templates is core navigation. Just below that, Icon-only Import/Export collapses Import and Export down to their icons instead of hiding either outright — a middle ground when you still want one-click access but not the label text; hover either icon for its name. Both are separate from, and finer-grained than, "Declutter app bar" (in the app bar's own right-click menu), which hides the title field, sidebar toggle, and Documents/Templates buttons instead — all of these can be combined
    • 

  • Toolbar — Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar: "Show toolbar" hides/shows the icon bar (also toggled by the floating button next to the editor or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + T); "Group labels" adds a caption under each icon group; "Word wrap" controls whether long lines wrap in the editor or scroll horizontally instead; "Expanded" shows the Extras group as buttons instead of a menu. "Sections" lets you hide whole icon groups (History, Structure, Move, Format, Fold, Insert, AI, Extras, Delete) for a leaner bar, and "Insert buttons" trims individual buttons within Insert without hiding the whole group
    • 

  • Editor's choice preset — Settings → Presets → Apply, or Quick Assist as a Run action ("editor's choice" — see below, also one of the clickable example phrases when Quick Assist opens empty): one click reconfigures the toolbar, sidebar, Pad, hover toolbar, status bar, app bar, Quick Assist location, editor backgrounds, Node Quick Assist, layout, and Presenter mode to a curated set for writing and presenting. Toolbar, sidebar, and Pad start hidden; hover toolbar is turned off; tree lines are hidden for a cleaner indented view; the status bar keeps only Words, Depth, Saved status, and the auto-rewrite indicator, hiding node count, max depth, node word count, the backup-connection chip, the Secure Storage lock chip, the theme chip, the text-size control, and the author field; the app bar stays visible with Quick Assist docked in it. Your accent color and node text color are left untouched — this preset is about density and visibility, not color. Note, Code block, and Pad all switch "Match editor background" off, so each reads as visually distinct chrome rather than blending into the outline canvas. The toolbar's own button set is curated for whenever you bring it back (Highlight, Text color, Collapse, Expand, Date/time, Decision log, Note, Code block, Tags, Rewrite, and Delete); the right-click quick row gets Focus, Rewrite, Note, Code block, Date/time, Decision log, Tags, and Delete — Focus lands there specifically because this preset turns off the hover toolbar, its usual home. Node Quick Assist (the Ctrl/Cmd+Space mid-edit menu) switches to its icon-only row and trims down to Note, Code block, Tags, Decision log, and Rewrite — the punctuation/symbol inserts drop out of that specific menu, not from anywhere else they're reachable. Presenter mode gets the laser pointer and branding turned on, "Open Preview straight into Presenter mode" turned on, and note cards set to expand automatically; the floating Presenter Notes panel's own toolbar is trimmed to Bullet list, Timestamp, and AI Rewrite. Everything not mentioned is hidden; you can still adjust any of this individually afterward in Settings, wherever that particular control normally lives. However you apply it, an Undo appears — Quick Assist's own toast gets one immediately, and Settings shows a persistent Undo button next to Apply that survives a page reload, so closing the toast or navigating away doesn't cost you the ability to revert. That undo restores every one of the settings above to what they were immediately before, in one step. Applying the preset again (from either entry point) replaces whatever undo point existed with a fresh one taken at that moment — it's a single-level undo, not a history
    • 

  • Documentation mode preset — Settings → Presets → Apply (second card, below Editor's choice), or Quick Assist as a Run action ("documentation mode"): the opposite instinct from Editor's choice, built for long-form structured documentation rather than presenting. Toolbar, sidebar, Pad, and status bar are all fully visible with nothing trimmed; hover toolbar is on; tree lines stay visible for navigating deep hierarchies; Node Quick Assist shows full labels with every action available. Decision Log, Tagging, and Backlinks are all switched on, with Decision Log entries shown with their status colors and bold field labels. Code block keeps "Match editor background" off so code stays visually distinct from the outline; Note and Pad blend in. Presenter mode's auto-behaviors (laser pointer, auto-launch, branding, auto-expanding note cards) are explicitly turned off — this preset isn't about presenting. Has its own separate Undo, independent of Editor's choice's — applying one doesn't clear the other's undo point
    • 

  • Hide tree lines — Settings → Layout → "Hide tree lines" (or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + L anytime): drops the │├└─ connector characters for a clean indented view, closer to Dynalist or Workflowy. Nodes with children keep their ▾/▸ fold arrow; childless nodes get a small dot in its place, so every row still has a marker. Indentation and folding behave identically either way — this only changes how the tree is drawn, not the structure itself. "Depth guide lines" (right below it, off by default) optionally brings back a faint sibling-connector line for a depth anchor once the branch connectors are gone
    • 

  • Section markings — Settings → Layout → "Section markings": toggles the coloured accent bar on section-header rows. On by default; independent of Hide tree lines
    • 

  • Row selection style — Settings → Layout → "Row selection style" controls how selected rows are marked: Fill (default) shades the row background, with the primary/anchor row a shade darker than the rest of a multi-select; Outline traces a thin border, with the anchor row's border and a light fill slightly stronger; Left bar shows an accent-coloured bar on the left edge, thicker on the anchor row; Dot marks each selected row with a small dot, larger and solid on the anchor row. Fill and Outline are neutral/near-neutral; Left bar and Dot use the active accent colour
    • 

  • Layout — Settings → Layout: "Compact rows" tightens spacing between separate rows independent of text size, without affecting line spacing within a single wrapped item; "Text size" is a slider from 85% to 140%; "Limit reading width" caps line length and centers the tree instead of spanning the full window, off by default (Preview has always had its own separate version of this, always on); "Branch spacing" sets the horizontal indent per depth level (2-6)
    • 

  • Sidebar — Settings → Bars & Menus → Sidebar: control whether new folders start collapsed, and whether a title-filter box appears above the documents list
    • 

  • All of the above are saved automatically and restored next time you open the app. "Reset to default settings" (Settings → Data) reverts appearance and behaviour preferences to their defaults without touching your documents
    • 

  


          
  

Structure
  

      

  • Right-click a node → a row of icon buttons for the most common actions; defaults are Zoom in, Note, Tags, Rewrite, Delete. Click More for move, sort, rewrite document, and everything else. Customise which buttons appear via Settings → Right-click menu — you can also reset to defaults there. Zoom in is also in the toolbar Fold group and via Ctrl/Cmd + .; zoom out via Ctrl/Cmd + , or clicking the same toolbar button again
    • 

  • Right-clicking empty space — editor canvas, sidebar, status bar, tab bar, or app bar — opens a separate, smaller menu of chrome-level actions, not the node menu above. Navigate it with ↓ / ↑ and Enter, same as the category picker above, or hover with a mouse — both move the same highlight. Editor canvas: switch theme, hide/show toolbar, sidebar, Pad, status bar, or tab bar, hide/show tree lines, "Show app bar" (only appears while zen mode has it hidden), Collapse all / Expand all, and Settings. Sidebar: switch theme, hide sidebar, toggle compact rows, Settings. Status bar: switch theme, hide status bar, Settings. Tab bar: switch theme, new document, close current tab, hide tab bar, Settings. App bar: switch theme, hide/show sidebar or toolbar, and "Declutter app bar" — which hides the title field, sidebar toggle, and the Documents/Templates buttons, but deliberately never hides Settings — hiding it would remove your only way back in. (Quick Assist used to share this exemption when it lived in the app bar too; it's since moved to the status bar, so it isn't part of this menu's options at all anymore.) Every hide action here shows an Undo toast, same pattern as Quick Assist's own toggles. If you hide Status bar or Tab bar from their own right-click menu, that specific spot disappears with it — but the editor canvas's own right-click menu keeps the same Status bar/Tab bar toggles, so it works as a fallback, in addition to the plain switches under Settings → Bars & Menus. Declutter app bar has no separate switch by design, since Settings (reachable via the button it deliberately never hides) already reverses it in one click
    • 

  • Backlinks — while editing a node, type @ to mention another node by name; a dropdown filters as you type, pick with mouse or arrow keys + Enter. The reference renders as a clickable link that jumps to the target. Open the Note panel on any node to see which nodes link to it. Deleting a referenced node removes its links from all other nodes automatically
    • 

  • Tags — select a node and click the tag icon (toolbar Insert group) or right-click → Tags to open the tag popover. Existing document tags appear as chips to toggle; type a new name and press Enter to add. Tags render as #chips on the node. Click a chip in the tree to filter to that tag; click again or × to clear. Multi-select: toolbar button applies to all selected nodes
    • 

  • Checkboxes — click the ☑ toolbar button (Insert group) to toggle a node as a checkbox. Or type [ ] / [x] at the start of a node while editing — it auto-converts on commit. Click the box to check/uncheck. Parent checkbox nodes with checkbox children show a 2/5 progress badge; checking all children auto-checks the parent. Checked nodes fade. Multi-select supported
    • 

  • ⤴ → insert above
    • 

  • ＋ → add child
    • 

  • Tab / Shift + Tab → indent / outdent
    • 

  • ⧉ → duplicate node / subtree
    • 

  • Alt + ↑ / ↓ → move
    • 

  • Drag a row → drop above/below to reorder, or onto the middle of another row to nest as its child
    • 

  • Right-click a node with children → More → Sort children (A → Z, Z → A, or by depth — shallowest subtree first)
    • 

  •  Extras in the toolbar → sort all top-level nodes the same three ways
    •   

  


                    


Tagging


    

  • Select a node and click the tag icon (toolbar Insert group) or right-click → Tags to open the tag popover. Existing document tags appear as chips to toggle; type a new name and press Enter to add. Remove a tag from a node by toggling its chip off in the popover
    • 

  • Tags render as #chips directly on the node row. Click a chip in the tree to filter the entire tree to that tag; click the chip again or the × to clear the filter
    • 

  • Multi-select: select several nodes, then click the tag icon — the popover applies to all selected nodes at once. Tags already on all selected nodes appear toggled on; tags on only some appear in an indeterminate state
    • 

  • AI Suggest tags (AI toolbar group) reads the node text and the document's existing tags, then applies relevant ones directly — prefers reusing tags already in the document before inventing new ones
    • 




          


@ Mentions & Backlinks


    

  • While editing a node, type @ to mention another node by name; a dropdown filters as you type — pick with mouse or arrow keys + Enter. The reference is saved as [[Node name]] and renders as a clickable link that jumps to the target node
    • 

  • Open the Note panel on any node to see its Backlinks section — every node that @mentions it appears there as a clickable entry. If no backlinks exist the section is hidden
    • 

  • Deleting a referenced node automatically removes its [[mention]] links from all other nodes
    • 

  • Turning the feature off (Settings → Panels → Note Panel → Backlinks) suppresses the @ dropdown and hides the Backlinks section in the note panel. Existing [[mentions]] in node text still render as clickable links regardless — they navigate on click even when the feature is off
    • 




          


Focus (zoom into a branch)


    

  • Right-click a node → Zoom in (or Ctrl/Cmd + ., or the zoom icon in the toolbar Fold group) → only that node and its descendants are shown. A breadcrumb bar appears above the editor showing the path back to the root; click any crumb to zoom out to that level
    • 

  • Exit focus with Ctrl/Cmd + ,, by clicking the same toolbar button again, or by clicking the root crumb in the breadcrumb bar
    • 

  • Focus state is per-tab and is not saved — closing and reopening a document always starts at the full tree
    • 

  • Turning the feature off (Settings → Editing → Focus) hides the zoom-in toolbar button and right-click action, and exits any active focus session immediately. Ctrl/Cmd + . also stops working while the feature is off
