    • 




          


Node hover toolbar


    

  • Hover any node to reveal two small buttons — Menu and Zoom in — right next to that row's own bullet, at any depth, including top-level nodes. They fade in only while you're hovering, and don't require selecting the node first
    • 

  • Zoom in acts immediately, same as right-click → Zoom in on branch (or Ctrl/Cmd + .) — see the Focus section above
    • 

  • Menu opens a vertical list of actions with icons and labels — Add child, Insert above, Insert below by default. Pick one to act on the node you were hovering; the list closes automatically afterward. Close it without choosing anything via Esc, clicking elsewhere, or moving to a different node
    • 

  • Settings → Editing → "Node hover toolbar" turns the whole thing on or off, right at the top of that section — "Menu items" below it is a checklist of everything the Menu dropdown can show — structural actions, AI actions, Note/Code/Tags/Decision log, and Delete. Zoom in isn't in that list since it already has its own dedicated button
    • 

  • The fold/unfold arrow (▾/▸) on any node with children turns into −/+ while you hover it, and clicking it works exactly the same either way
    • 

  • Hovering the tree connector or the leaf dot at the start of a node — not the label text — shows a themed tooltip with when it was created and last modified. It's a separate control from the hover toolbar above; turn it off independently via Settings → Layout → "Timestamps on hover"
    • 





  

Formatting
  

      

  • Ctrl/Cmd + B → bold
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + I → italic
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + U → underline
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + S → strike
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + H → highlight (caret next to it picks a color)
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + F → text color (caret next to it picks a color)
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + 1–6 → apply Heading 1–6 to the selected node(s), independent of tree depth — pick from the dropdown next to Text color, or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + 0 / re-clicking the same level to return to body text. Combines with bold/italic/underline/strike on top if you want further emphasis
    •   

  


          


Semantics


    

  • [Text] → Section — blue label
    • 

  • (Text) → note — muted italic
    • 

  • !Text → alert — red warning
    • 

  • `Text` → code — inline code
    • 

  • SAP Note 123456 → SAP Note 123456 — auto-linked to me.sap.com, opens in a new tab. Also recognizes "OSS Note", case-insensitive. No markup to type — just write it naturally
    • 

  • >Text → quoted or attributed content — starts the node with >; hides the > in view mode. Use for direct quotes, attributed statements, or cited requirements
    • 

  • @Node name → Node name — backlink to another node; type @ while editing to pick from a list. Open the Note panel on the target node to see all backlinks pointing to it
    • 

  • [ ] task or [x] task → auto-converts to a checkbox node on commit
    • 

  • Tag icon in toolbar or right-click → Tags → tag popover (toggle existing tags or add new ones)
    • 




          


AI Rewrite


    

  • ✦ button in the toolbar → rewrites the selected node; with multiple nodes selected, rewrites all of them in one batch; with text highlighted inside an editing node, rewrites just that selection
    • 

  • Right-click a node → Rewrite node is in the quick icon row (Rewrite selection, if multiple are selected); click More → Rewrite document to rewrite every node in the tree
    • 

  •  button next to Rewrite, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + O → Generate outline (AI): describe a topic and it inserts an AI-generated nested outline into the current document — into a blank doc, or after the current selection; also available as "Generate with AI" on the blank-document screen. Uses the same provider/model/key as Rewrite (Settings → AI → Provider), but its own fixed instructions, not the customizable rewrite prompt
    • 

  • Restructure text (AI) — Import ▾ → "Restructure text", or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + R: paste messy or unstructured text (notes, an email, a transcript) and it organizes it into a tree, cleaning up wording as needed (fixing broken line wraps, tightening long sentences) so it reads well as outline nodes — it will not invent facts that are not in your text, but it is not a verbatim copy either. If the pasted text already has clear structure (bullets, consistent indentation, or literal tree-connector characters), it is parsed directly with no AI call at all. Unlike Generate outline, this always opens as a new document rather than inserting into the one you have open, so an import can never accidentally merge into unrelated work — your original pasted text is kept in that new document's Pad so you can check it against the result
    • 

  • Word import — Import ▾ → "Word (.docx)": converts the file's own heading and list structure directly into nodes — no AI involved when the document actually uses heading styles, since that structure is already explicit in the file. If a document has no heading styles at all (just a wall of plain paragraphs), its text is automatically handed to the same AI restructuring used above instead of being imported as one long flat list. Tables import as a node per row with each column as a child node; images are dropped but left as a "[image]" placeholder so nothing vanishes silently. Always opens as a new document, with the extracted text kept in its Pad. Legacy .doc files (pre-2007 binary format) are not supported — only modern .docx
    • 

  • Word export — Export ▾ → "Word (.docx)": a genuine, editable document — real heading styles (so Word's own navigation pane and outline view work), an updatable Table of Contents with clickable page references (right-click → Update Field if headings change later), and Decision Log entries rendered as bordered/shaded cards with the same accent-colored status and field labels as Preview. Notes carry through with their actual formatting — bold, italic, underline, strike, and links all become real Word formatting rather than flattening to plain text; bullet/numbered lists inside a note get a simple prefix treatment, and a table inside a note flattens to one line per row (cells joined by " | ") rather than a real Word table grid. Images marked "Feature as diagram" (right-click an image in a note) embed as real pictures, same as PowerPoint export, scaled to fit while keeping their aspect ratio; a diagram that can't be read or converted is skipped with the rest of the document still exporting normally. Diagrams created in the Pad's Diagrams tab and linked to a node embed the same way, controlled by Settings → Export & print → "Include Diagrams in exports". Settings → Export & print → "Cover page" (on by default, shared with PDF) adds a title page — document title, a divider rule, author, and a word count/read time/decisions-logged line — before the Table of Contents. Follows Preview/Presenter's own fold state (like PDF and PowerPoint) rather than Settings → Export & print → "Skip folded sections" — whichever sections are currently folded there are what's left out. Settings → Preview → Presenter mode → "Branding" adds the same small corner mark used in PowerPoint export and the Presenter bar, as a running page footer, plus a wordmark above the title on the cover page itself
    • 

  • PowerPoint export — Export ▾ → "PowerPoint (.pptx)": builds a genuine, editable slide deck using the same slide breakdown as Preview's Presenter mode (Settings → Preview → Presenter mode → "Presenter slide breaks at" controls this) — a title slide, then one slide per node at that depth, its direct children as bullet text, and any images marked "Feature as diagram" (right-click an image in a note) — or diagrams from the Pad's Diagrams tab linked to that node — embedded as pictures sized to fit, correctly scaled to their real aspect ratio. Everything opens fully editable in PowerPoint, Keynote, or Google Slides — text boxes, bullets, and pictures are normal native shapes, not a flattened image of the slide. Regular note text (not marked as a diagram) isn't included, since a slide with a diagram plus a full note's worth of prose would be unreadably dense — trim the note to what belongs on the slide via the bullets themselves if you want more control over what's on it. Whichever sections are currently folded in Preview/Presenter (not the main outline editor, and not Settings → Export & print → "Skip folded sections") are what's left out of the deck — fold or expand a section there first if you want to change what exports. Settings → Preview → Presenter mode → "Branding" (off by default) adds a small mark to the bottom-right corner of every slide — "SAKURA" by default, or your own company/team name if set in "Branding text" right below it — the same mark and setting used in the Presenter bar, and in PDF and Word exports too
    • 

  •  button next to Outline → Suggest icon: picks a fitting emoji for the selected node's label and adds it as a prefix (e.g. "🛒 Webshop"). With multiple nodes selected, applies one straight away to each, picked from a keyword list first and an exact match against icons already used elsewhere in your documents/templates, falling to AI only when neither matches. For a single node, if more than one good option turns up, a small popover appears above it to pick from; right-click → More → Suggest icons for all nodes runs it over the whole tree (asks to confirm first). Since the icon is just leading text, removing or changing it is normal editing — double-click the node and edit it like any other text
    • 

  • Batches larger than 30 nodes are sent in sequential chunks automatically, with a status toast showing progress (e.g. "Rewriting nodes 1–30…")
    • 

  • Expand node (AI) — select a single node and click the expand button in the AI toolbar group to break it into child nodes. Useful for dense labels like "Distribution model (OPML, localStorage, multi-tab sync)" → a proper subtree. AI generates 3–8 children; result is undoable
    • 

  • Suggest tags (AI) — select a node and click the tag-suggest button; AI reads the node text and the document's existing tags, then applies relevant ones directly. Prefers reusing tags already in the document before inventing new ones
    • 

  • Summarise selection into parent (AI) — select 2 or more nodes (Shift-click or Ctrl/Cmd-click), then click the summarise button; AI writes a concise parent label and inserts it above the selection, with the selected nodes indented underneath. The bottom-up outlining pattern — build the detail first, name the group after
    • 

  • Settings → AI → Provider → choose a provider from the dropdown, paste the API key for it, then head to the Rewrite section to optionally customise the rewrite prompt. All AI features — Rewrite, Expand, Tag suggestion, Summarise, Generate outline, Suggest icon — share the same active provider and key. "Reset to default" next to the prompt box restores the original grammar-and-spelling-only wording; "Rephrase" swaps in a different canned prompt that's allowed to restructure sentences and vary wording, not just correct errors — both replace the box's contents and save immediately, the same way, so review before relying on either. There's only one active prompt: switching to Rephrase also changes what Auto-rewrite on commit does, if it's on, since auto-rewrite always uses whichever prompt is currently active. "Reset AI settings" at the bottom restores provider, model, and prompt together and clears every saved key
    • 

  • Auto-rewrite on commit (Settings → AI → Auto-rewrite on commit, off by default) — runs Rewrite automatically on a node as soon as you finish editing it, no manual click needed. Only text typed by hand counts: if any part of an edit session came from a paste or drop (checked via the browser's own paste/drop signal on that edit), that node is skipped for auto-rewrite on that commit — paste it, then use the manual Rewrite button if you want AI on it. Committing a node with no actual text change (e.g. clicking in and back out without editing) is also skipped — it doesn't re-queue nodes that haven't changed. It doesn't send one request per node: committed nodes are queued and sent as a single batched request once either an idle pause passes or enough nodes have queued up, whichever comes first — both the pause length (1-300s, default 120s) and the queue size (2-50, default 5) are configurable in Settings, not fixed — built this way specifically to avoid exhausting provider rate limits during continuous note-taking. A small ✦ dot appears next to any node currently queued (or mid-batch, waiting on the response) — click that dot to pull just that node back out of the queue before it's sent — separate from the status bar chip, which shows the overall off / idle / queued-count-with-a-live-countdown / paused / rewriting state and doubles as the control itself: click it to turn auto-rewrite on or off directly (see below for what clicking does instead while it's paused), right-click it for its settings (exclusions, minimum word count, idle pause, queue size). The countdown ticks down to the batch's send time and resets to the full idle-pause value every time another node joins the queue — a fresh edit means a fresh wait, not a shorter one. Both the queued count and the countdown are independently hideable from the chip (Settings → AI → Auto-rewrite on commit, below the queue-size setting) if either feels like more detail than you want at a glance — the chip still distinguishes "something's queued" from idle even with both turned off, just without the numbers; the tooltip on hover always shows the full detail regardless of what the chip itself displays. Exclusions (checkboxes, decision-log field lines, headings, [[backlinks]]/`code` syntax) and the minimum word count are each independently configurable checkboxes in Settings, not fixed. Needs an API key configured above to do anything; if none is available right now — none set, or Secure Storage is locked so the saved one can't be read — the chip turns red and shows "paused," queued nodes are held rather than discarded, and the batch sends automatically the moment a key becomes usable again (e.g. right after unlocking) rather than waiting for the next edit to retrigger it. Clicking the chip while paused acts as the fix — unlocks Secure Storage directly if that's the cause, or jumps to the key field in Settings if not. That's separate from the retry-exhaustion case: if it fails three times in a row with a key that is available (e.g. an expired key), it turns itself off and tells you why, rather than silently retrying forever. Switching documents discards anything still queued rather than rewriting into the wrong document. Nodes created by Restructure text, Generate outline, paste-a-tree, and Word/OPML import are never queued for auto-rewrite regardless of this setting — only nodes you type into interactively go through commitEdit at all
    • 

  • Built-in providers — six providers ship with Sakura: Gemini (free), Groq (free, fast), Claude API, OpenRouter (free models), Cerebras (free tier), and GitHub Models (free, needs your GitHub account). All need an API key — selecting a provider shows a direct link to where you get one. GitHub Models requires a GitHub personal access token with models:read scope; model IDs in the dropdown already include the required vendor prefix (e.g. openai/gpt-4o). Built-ins can’t be edited or deleted
    • 

  • Provider fallback — Settings → AI → Provider → Fallback order: enable the toggle to let Sakura automatically retry with the next checked provider when the active one fails — quota, rate limit, wrong model ID, or any other error. The active provider is always tried first; the list sets the retry order. Providers without a saved key are skipped automatically, and if none of the checked providers besides the active one have a key, a notice under the list says so directly rather than letting it fail silently the first time it's actually needed. A toast names both the failed provider and the one that succeeded (e.g. “Quota hit on Groq — rewritten with Gemini ✦” or “Error on Groq — rewritten with Gemini ✦”). The only error that does not trigger fallback is a bad API key (401) — that surfaces directly so you know to update it. Default order on a fresh install: Groq → Gemini → Cerebras → OpenRouter, with the first three enabled
    • 

  • To-Dos AI — Settings → AI → To-Dos AI, three independent toggles (each on by default): Extract action items adds a button in the To-Dos panel header that reads the current document — or your selected nodes, if any are selected — and proposes a batch of tasks from it, added directly with one Undo for the whole batch. Break into subtasks adds a small AI button on each open task (next to Rewrite) that proposes 3-6 concrete steps and adds them as real subtasks, also with Undo; it's hidden on tasks that already have subtasks or are set to repeat, since those can't hold subtasks. Status summary adds a header button that writes a short plain-language readout of your open tasks — what's overdue or high-priority first, then general progress — shown in a dialog with a Copy button. All three use the same provider/key/model as Rewrite above and need AI Capabilities on (Settings → Features); turning a toggle off here removes just that one button, leaving Rewrite and the other two untouched
    • 

  • Usage today — below the API key field, Sakura counts requests made to the currently selected provider today (and shows the count next to each provider in the Fallback order list once any requests are made). This is a local, best-effort counter — it resets on your computer's local calendar day, which can drift slightly from a provider's own reset clock (e.g. Gemini resets at midnight Pacific), and it isn't fetched from the provider itself. Treat it as a rough gauge of how much you've used a provider today, not an authoritative quota reading — check the provider's own dashboard (e.g. Google AI Studio) for the real number if it matters
    • 

  • Test key — the Test button next to Save sends a minimal request (asks the model to reply "OK") to confirm the currently typed or saved key actually works, without waiting for your next real Rewrite to find out. A toast reports success or the specific error. This check does not count toward the Usage today total above, since it's a connectivity check rather than real feature use
    • 

  • Custom providers — "+ Add provider" connects any OpenAI-compatible, Gemini-style, or Anthropic-style API by name and base URL — no code changes. User-added providers can be edited or deleted; built-ins cannot. Each provider remembers its own last-used model independently
    • 




          
  

Utilities
  

      

  • Ctrl/Cmd + S → save now (kept in-app — doesn't trigger the browser's "Save Page As")
    • 

  • Print button in the header (next to Import ▾ / Export ▾) → prints the document with all UI chrome hidden; choose "Save as PDF" in the browser's print dialog to get a clean PDF. Checked nodes and tag chips render correctly in print; the sidebar, toolbar, and status bar are all hidden automatically
    •   

  • Export ▾ is grouped into Copy / Document / Data sections. Markdown, OPML, Text, and Print all respect Settings → Export & print → "Skip folded sections" the same way — folded branches are included or skipped consistently across all of them, not just whichever one you happen to use. PDF, PowerPoint, and Word are the exception: all three render from (or mirror) Preview/Presenter, which has its own independent fold state you control directly by clicking a section to expand or collapse it there — whatever that's currently set to is exactly what goes into the PDF, deck, or document, regardless of "Skip folded sections"
    • 

  • Import ▾ (next to Export ▾) is grouped into "From a file" (OPML, Word) and "From pasted text" (Restructure text, AI) — see the AI section above for how Restructure text and Word import handle structure
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + D / Insert Date/Time in toolbar → timestamp
    •   

  • Copy Tree as Image → PNG
    •   

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + C → copy selection
    •   

  • Clear → reset
    •   

  • Shortcuts that double as OS/browser bindings can occasionally be intercepted before they reach the page — if one doesn't fire, the matching button or menu item always works
    •   

  


          


Maximize / Zen mode


    

  • ⤢ floating button bottom-right of the editor, Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + M to toggle it on or off, or Esc to exit only → by default hides the sidebar and app bar, so only the tree, toolbar (if it's on), tab bar, and a trimmed status bar remain
    • 

  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide File Explorer (sidebar)" — on by default, so the sidebar auto-collapses when you enter zen mode. The header's own sidebar-toggle button (▤) keeps working normally throughout zen mode regardless of this setting, so you can always open or close it manually for that session — same "manual choice sticks until you exit" behavior as the toolbar and Pad below
    • 

  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide status bar" controls whether the bottom status bar is hidden outright on entry — off by default. Left on, zen mode instead trims it down to just Words, Depth, Saved status, and the auto-rewrite indicator (plus Quick Assist, if it's normally docked in the app bar — see below), regardless of your normal status bar item selection, and restores your normal selection when you exit
    • 

  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide tab bar" controls whether the row of open document tabs is hidden on entry — off by default, so you can switch between open documents without leaving zen mode. Turn it on if you'd rather it disappear too
    • 

  • A second floating button sits next to it — a shortcut for Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar → "Show toolbar," so it works the same way whether you're in zen mode or not. Click it, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + T, to show or hide the toolbar; the choice applies everywhere and is remembered next time
    • 

  • If you'd rather zen mode hide the toolbar by default, turn "Show toolbar" off before maximizing — everything it holds is still reachable by keyboard shortcut (see the other sections on this page) or by right-clicking a node for the icon row and "More" menu
    • 

  • The Pad panel has its own floating toggle next to the maximize button, since it stays open across zen mode
    • 

  • The Pad has three sides, switched via tabs in its header (Notepad / Q&A / Decision Log) — same panel, same open/close/position, just a different view. The Q&A tab is a structured list: each row is a bold Question and a regular-weight Answer; hover either one for its own small Rewrite (AI) button, hover the row for its delete icon. A running "n/n answered" count sits in the toolbar. The scan icon scans the outline for nodes ending in "?" and offers them as a checklist — pre-checked if you had question nodes selected first, otherwise all of them; confirming adds them as new rows, and rescanning is safe since questions already added show disabled and unchecked instead of duplicating. Right next to it, the AI generate button (Settings → AI → Q&A AI → "Generate questions") reads the document and suggests questions worth asking about it — gaps, ambiguities, things it implies but doesn't actually answer — rather than only picking up nodes that literally end in "?"; it's selection-aware, so with a node (or a multi-selection of several) selected in the outline it scopes to just that section rather than the whole document, which also helps on longer documents where feeding everything at once dilutes what the AI focuses on. It opens the same review checklist as Scan, so nothing's added until you confirm, and these rows have no source-node link since they're new questions rather than ones already written into the outline. The Import button opens a paste box for bringing in Q&A from outside Sakura — it understands "Q:"/"A:" pairs (including Sakura's own Q&A text export, so re-importing an exported .md file round-trips cleanly) and falls back to treating every "?"-ending line as a standalone question if there are no Q/A markers at all; it opens that same review checklist too. The toolbar groups these three (Import/Scan/Generate) together as "bring in content from elsewhere," set apart from the + button and its neighbor — one blank row, one section/topic header — which are for writing your own from scratch. New rows and sections both land at the end of the list regardless of where you're looking, so drag the grip to move a row under the section it belongs to, or hover a section row for a small "+" that inserts a new blank question at the end of that section directly, without a drag. Sections drag and delete like any other row but have no select checkbox of their own — deliberately, since a group heading isn't something you'd bulk-act on the same way a batch of real rows is — and don't count toward the answered total, and are dropped from view (rather than left as an empty label) whenever a filter or search leaves nothing under them. Past the filter/search group, the whole-list AI actions run in the order a person would actually use them — Group into sections first (Settings → AI → Q&A AI → "Group into sections": reads every question and organizes them into a small number of named sections in one pass, replacing any existing grouping with a fresh one built from scratch, though every question keeps its answer, follow-up flag, and Answered by; only the ordering and section headers change, and Undo restores the previous arrangement in one step), then Draft answers, then Summary. A row added from a scan keeps a small link icon (hover the question) that jumps straight to that node in the outline; the icon disappears on its own if that node is later deleted, and when that happens an orange "n unlinked" chip appears next to the answered count — click it to show just those rows, the same way the answered count filters to unanswered ones (the two filters can combine). Each row also has a small chip (hover to reveal, stays visible once set) for flagging an answer as not yet trustworthy: click an unflagged row to mark it "Follow-up" (amber) for your own uncertain answers, or let AI-drafted answers flag themselves automatically as "AI draft" (accent-colored) — either way the chip only clears when you click it yourself, so editing the answer text doesn't silently mark it reviewed. A matching "n follow-up" chip in the toolbar filters to both flagged types together, same as the other two chips, and all three filters can combine. Once a row has an answer, a small "Answered by…" field and a date appear alongside the follow-up chip — hover-only until a name's actually been typed in, same reveal-on-hover treatment as the follow-up chip, so an empty one doesn't sit on screen for every single answered row; once it holds a name it stays visible like any other row data. The date is set automatically whenever the answer changes, but who answered is always typed in by hand, since it's frequently someone else (a colleague from business or IT) rather than whoever's typing it into Sakura; recently-used names show up as autocomplete suggestions so repeat entries are quick without ever being assumed. Selecting several rows (see below) adds a bulk "Set Answered by" button next to bulk Rewrite — one name, applied to every selected row that already has an answer (skipping any that don't, rather than refusing the whole batch), with its own Undo. Drag the grip on the left to reorder rows by hand, or click the "n/n answered" count to show only unanswered rows — the two don't mix: dragging is unavailable while that filter is active, since a hand-reorder inside a filtered view has no single sensible "real" position to save it at. Deleting a row — one at a time, or several at once — shows an Undo toast for a few seconds rather than deleting silently. Hover a row for a small select checkbox (Shift-click for a range), or use the checkbox above the list to select or deselect everything; once anything's selected, a delete icon appears next to that same header checkbox for a batch delete, also with Undo. Escape clears the current selection and Delete/Backspace removes it, both only while focus isn't inside a question or answer field, so they never interfere with normal typing. Since Q&A lives in the same Pad element that Presenter Notes relocates, it flips right along with it — press Q in Presenter mode to open Presenter Notes straight into the Q&A view (N for Notepad) and work through questions without leaving fullscreen. Settings → Editing → Pad has "Include Q&A in exports" (Word and PDF append it as a final section — bold question, regular-weight answer, unanswered rows marked "No answer provided" — turn off to keep Q&A in the Pad only)
    • 

  • While actually presenting (not just browsing Preview), right-clicking a line's actual text opens a small popover built for capturing live feedback without breaking presenting flow — left-click always just advances to the next slide now, same as clicking anywhere else (matching the standard PowerPoint/Keynote/Google Slides convention with no exceptions); right-clicking anywhere that isn't a line's text still opens the laser-pointer toggle menu exactly as before. Four quick marks — ✓ Confirmed, ✗ Issue, ⏸ Parked, 🚩 Follow-up — click one to set it, click the same one again to clear it; a small colored glyph then shows on that line, both in Preview/Presenter and back in the editor's own outline, for the rest of the session (click the glyph in the editor to clear it from there too). Next to the marks, Edit turns that line's own text into a plain editable field right on the slide, in place — cursor already at the end, no popover involved at all; Enter or clicking away saves it straight back to the node, Escape reverts to what it said before. It's the same underlying text the editor and outline both use, so it's picked up everywhere the next time anything re-renders. Note… and Question… swap the same small popover's content in place for whichever one you picked instead — rather than opening a separate full-screen prompt; for something that's genuinely a line or two, a modal taking over the whole screen was more interruption than the content warranted. Each shows a compact header line (which one you're in, on the left; icon-only Cancel and Save on the right — Enter also saves, Escape also cancels) above a plain, borderless field, and both start blank and fold a short preview of which node you clicked into that same header line (label · node text), so you can confirm you've got the right one before typing without needing a whole extra row for it. Note appends a timestamped line to that node's Note field — if it already has note content, a Delete icon appears next to Save so you can clear the whole note in one action instead of only ever adding to it. Question adds a new Q&A row linked to that node — neither Note nor Question copies the node's own text into what gets saved, only what you actually type; the Q&A row links back via the same mechanism a Scan-created row uses for its own link icon, so nothing about the outline itself gets duplicated. Either way, a small indicator dot appears on that line (both in Preview/Presenter and back in the editor's own outline) — the same note-dot an ordinary note gets, or a dedicated question-mark dot for a Question — so a captured line stays visibly marked rather than only living inside the Pad; click either dot to jump straight to what was captured
    • 

  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide app bar" also hides the very top row (Documents/Templates, search, Settings, Help, Import/Export) while zen — on by default. If Quick Assist is normally docked in the app bar ("Dock in app bar" in Settings → Quick Assist), zen mode relocates it into the status bar for as long as zen mode is on instead, rather than popping the app bar back open every time you invoke it; your "Dock in app bar" preference itself is untouched and Quick Assist returns to the app bar when you exit zen mode. If the app bar is hidden, right-click the editor canvas for a "Show app bar" option (only appears while it's actually hidden), or use the keyboard shortcuts for whatever you need — everything the app bar holds still works that way too
    • 

  • Settings → Zen mode → "Use browser fullscreen" — on by default — additionally requests the browser's own fullscreen (the same as pressing F11) when you enter zen mode, hiding the browser's tabs and address bar too, not just Sakura's own UI. Exiting fullscreen — Esc, F11, or the browser's own control — exits zen mode at the same time, so the two never end up out of sync
    • 




          


Preview


    

  • 👁 floating button bottom-right of the editor (next to zen/Pad/toolbar) → opens a read-only, presentation-style render of the current document. Nothing here can be edited, and closing it never changes your actual document
    • 

  • Headings come from the Heading 1–6 style you apply in the editor (Format toolbar, next to Text color) — not from tree depth. A node with no heading style of its own renders as plain body text in Preview, unless Settings → Preview → "Headings & Contents" has a level assigned to that node's depth (see below)
    • 

  • Settings → Preview → "Headings & Contents" → "Heading fallback by depth" lets you assign a heading level to depths 0–5, so an unstyled node can still render as a heading in Preview based purely on how deep it sits in the tree. An explicit heading style on the node always wins over this; depths left as "None" behave exactly as before. This only changes how Preview renders — it never writes a heading style into the actual document
    • 

  • Table of contents (left panel, toggled with the header button) is built from headings and [Section] markers — what it includes is configurable in Settings → Preview → "Headings & Contents": cap it to a maximum heading level, or turn off section markers to keep it to real headings only. "Treat depth 0 as section markers" there (off by default) makes every top-level node act as a [Section] divider automatically, without needing the [Bracket] text — [Bracket] text still works at any depth on top of this, including depth 0. Unlike the Table of Contents toggle, this one changes the document itself: the outline canvas, Preview/PDF, and rich-text copy all pick up the same section styling, not just what's listed in Contents. Below the headings, a "Decisions" section lists every decision log in the document with a colored status dot — click to jump straight to it. The panel shows itself if there are 2+ headings or at least 1 decision log, whichever applies; it's part of what gets printed to PDF, so the decision index carries over there too
    • 

  • Presenter mode (▶ button in the header) goes fullscreen and hides all the header buttons — the bottom bar (prev/next, slide counter, exit) is the only control surface while presenting. Opens on a decorated title slide (document title, author, the same word-count/decision-count line as the Preview header) before stepping through the rest one section at a time — arrow keys/space to navigate, clicking the slide itself also advances (same as PowerPoint/Keynote), Home/End to jump to first/last, Esc (or leaving fullscreen any other way) to exit. Fold state, notes, code blocks, and decision cards all render normally within each slide. A running elapsed timer sits in the bar. G opens a grid of every slide's label — click one to jump straight to it, useful for Q&A on longer decks; Esc closes the grid without exiting presenter mode. B blacks out the screen to pull attention back to you mid-discussion — press again (or Esc) to resume exactly where you were, nothing about the slide itself changes. N opens a floating Notepad panel without leaving Presenter mode — Q opens that same panel straight into its Q&A view instead (pressing the key for whichever view is already showing closes the panel; the other key switches views without closing it) — it's the same Pad that document already has (Settings → Editing → Pad), the real element (editor and toolbar both) just moves into view and back, so whatever you jot down while presenting is that document's Pad afterward, nothing separate to lose track of; needs Pad enabled and a document open. The panel is movable and resizable — drag its header to reposition it anywhere on screen, drag the small grip in its bottom-right corner to resize it, double-click either one to snap back to the default docked position — whatever you leave it at is remembered next time you open it, this session and future ones. Separately, per-node notes (the small dot that appears on a node with a note attached) stay collapsed on their slide until clicked — clicking one opens it in a popover without leaving Presenter mode. That's a different feature from the floating Notepad panel above it — despite both involving the word "note," one is the whole-document Pad floating over your slides, the other is notes attached to specific nodes and rendered inline within them. The floating panel shows its own, smaller set of toolbar buttons — Bullet list and AI Rewrite by default — independent of the full toolbar the regular Pad panel shows; pick a different set in Settings → Preview → Presenter mode → Toolbar buttons while presenting. Timer, the all-slides grid, and notes can each be turned off in Settings → Preview → Presenter mode, along with which tree depth starts a new slide, auto-enabling the laser pointer, "Presenter zoom" (scales slide content while presenting, separate from the regular editor's text size — defaults to 150%, since slide content generally reads better larger from across a room than a document read up close), "Open Preview straight into Presenter mode" (skips the regular scrolling Preview — opens straight to the title slide, fullscreen, and Esc then takes you all the way back to the editor in one press instead of backing out one layer at a time), and a small optional branding mark in the presenter bar (and, from the same setting, in the corner of exported PowerPoint slides) — "SAKURA" by default, or your own company/team name via "Branding text" — off by default
    • 

  • Settings → Preview → "Reading appearance" adds reading width, a sepia reading theme, and a serif body-text option — Preview-only, doesn't touch the editor or document. Settings → Data & Backup → "Export & print" → "PDF page margins" sets the default Export as PDF starts with; the print dialog that opens still lets you change it per export
    • 

  • Click ▾/▸ → fold/unfold a branch, same as the editor — but this fold state is local to Preview and separate from the editor's own collapsed sections. Collapse all / Expand all buttons sit in the header
    • 

  • Folding a branch that contains a note or a decision log doesn't hide it silently — a small icon next to the +N badge tells you it's in there; click either to expand
    • 

  • Notes render as their real rich content (images, lists, links) inside a card, not the small dot from the editor
    • 

  • Decision logs become a card with a "Decision Log" header — Status always shows there as a colored badge — including its unedited default of "Proposed", since that's a real status rather than a placeholder question (color is guessed from the word itself: approved/accepted/implemented/resolved/done/confirmed → green, rejected/declined/blocked/abandoned/cancelled → red, hold/pending/review → orange, anything else → gray). Each other answered field gets its own labeled block below, in order — and if you've written the actual answer as child nodes under a field (e.g. a numbered list under Rationale, or bullet points under Impact) rather than on the field's own line, that nested content renders as a list inside the block, numbered automatically if your items start with "1.", "2.", etc. A field only shows if it has real content — either its own line has been edited past the default question, or it has children. Which fields appear at all is configurable in Settings → Preview → "Decision card fields" (Context + Decision by default; this only affects how Preview summarizes a log, not what the Decision Log feature itself inserts)
    • 

  • Right-click anywhere in Preview → Laser pointer, for pointing at things while presenting without your real cursor in the way. Turns off automatically when you close Preview
    • 

  • Export buttons in the header (PDF / Word) are the same export as everywhere else in Sakura — Preview just gives you a one-click path to them while you're already looking at the rendered result
    • 




          


Large trees


    

  • Click ▾/▸ → fold / unfold subtree
    • 

  • ← / → → collapse / expand selected
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd + F → search this tree; Ctrl/Cmd + H → search and replace (Replace / Replace all, undoable)
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + C / ▶ in toolbar → collapse all (folds every top-level section)
    • 

  • Same shortcut / same button again → expand all — it's a single toggle, the icon flips to show which one it'll do next
    • 




          


Documents & folders (sidebar)


    

  •  button in sidebar header → new folder;  button → new document (unfiled); or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + N from anywhere
    • 

  • Hover a folder → ＋ creates a document already filed there; ✎ renames it;  deletes it (subfolders move up a level, documents move to Unfiled — Undo appears briefly after deleting)
    • 

  • Right-click a folder → new document or new subfolder here, rename, or delete
    • 

  • Right-click a document → rename, duplicate, move to a folder, or move to Trash
    • 

  • Click ✎ / ⧉ /  icons on a document row → rename / duplicate / move to Trash; the folder-icon button moves it via a dropdown list
    • 

  • Click a document's icon (left of its name) → pick from a small set of icons to mark it visually, or "Use default icon" to clear it back to the plain page icon. Purely visual, doesn't affect the document's content
    • 

  • Click a document's or folder's name → switch to it / expand it
    • 

  • Folders can be nested: drag a folder onto another folder's top or bottom edge to reorder it as a sibling there, or onto the middle to nest it inside
    • 

  • Drag a document onto a folder (or "Unfiled") to file it there; drag it onto another document to reorder within that folder/Unfiled
    • 

  •  /  buttons by "Documents" → collapse all / expand all folders (only shown once a folder exists)
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd-click a document to multi-select; Shift-click selects a range. A bar appears to move or trash the whole selection at once
    • 

  • Deleting a document (or a selection of them) — or a template, including a built-in one — moves it to Trash, a collapsible section at the very bottom of the sidebar, below Templates — out of the way since it's rarely needed, same as most apps put it. It's not gone. Each item there shows when it was trashed, with buttons to restore it or delete it forever; documents and templates sit in the same list, told apart by their icon and a small "Template" label. A toast with "Undo" also appears right after trashing, for a quick reversal without opening Trash at all. Settings → Trash controls how long items sit there before being purged automatically (default 7 days; "Forever" turns off auto-purge so only "Empty Trash" removes anything) — one setting, shared by both. Header search (Ctrl/Cmd + Space) skips Trash by default — turn on Settings → Trash → "Include Trash in search" to have it surface matches there too (across documents, templates, node text, Notes, tags, and the Pad alike), clearly marked "In Trash"; clicking one reveals it in the Trash section rather than opening it directly, since restoring is a deliberate choice. Settings → Data → "Restore built-in templates" also pulls a trashed built-in back out of Trash, not just refreshes its content
    • 

  • Searching the sidebar keeps folders visible (showing only matching documents and subfolders, force-expanded) but disables drag-and-drop while a search is active; it only matches document/template names — for text inside documents, use the header search (Ctrl/Cmd + Space) instead
    • 

  • The sidebar's filter box is hidden by default since the header search now covers the same ground; bring it back via the  icon next to "Documents", or Settings → Bars & Menus → Sidebar → Filter box
    • 

  • Drag the thin strip at the sidebar's right edge to resize it
    • 

  • Version history (right-click a document → "Version history", or "More" → "Version history" for the one currently open) keeps automatic checkpoints of a document's past content — not full version control, just lightweight snapshots. A version is captured right before it would otherwise be overwritten, but only once every ~10 minutes of active editing at most, and only if something actually changed, so it won't flood with near-duplicates from every autosave tick. Capped at the last 20 per document, oldest dropped first. "Save a version now" in that panel adds one immediately, useful right before a big rewrite. Restoring an older version is itself non-destructive — whatever was there gets saved as a version first, so restoring is never a one-way trip
    • 

  • If the same document is open as the active one in two tabs or windows of this browser at once (easy to do by accident — duplicating a tab, browser session restore, opening the file twice), a banner appears the moment the other one saves a change, since otherwise whichever tab saves last would silently win with no warning. "Reload latest" discards anything unsaved here and pulls in the other tab's version; dismissing it just clears the banner — your edits here are kept either way, and the other tab gets the same warning in reverse once you save. Documents open here only in the background (not the one on screen) refresh silently instead, since there's nothing unsaved there to protect. This only applies within one browser — a completely different browser, or a different device, never shares storage and can't collide
    • 




          


Templates (sidebar)


    

  • Click a template's name in the Templates section → load it into the current document (replaces current content; Undo restores it)
    • 

  •  Default → loads your custom default template, if one is set
    • 

  • ⋯ button → Save current as template, or Set current as default
    • 

  •  button next to ⋯ → new template folder, for grouping templates (single level; templates can be moved between folders via the move-to-folder button or right-click, but folders themselves can't be nested or reordered)
    • 

  •  /  buttons by "Templates" → collapse all / expand all template folders (only shown once a template folder exists)
    • 

  • Click a template's icon → same small icon-picker as documents, to mark it visually. Built-in templates keep their fixed icon (grouped by document shape) and aren't customizable this way
    • 

  • Ships with a built-in pack — Decision Record, plus a set of SAP architecture templates — grouped in a "Built-in" folder. (Meeting-style templates like Meeting Notes, 1:1s, Daily Standup, Weekly Status, Project Kickoff, and Retrospective live inside the Meetings panel's own "New from template" menu instead, since they fill in a meeting note rather than insert outline nodes.) The folder and the templates in it can be renamed and moved like any other, but are deleted like any other — deletion goes to Trash and is reversible. Use the ↺ restore button in the Templates sidebar header to restore any deleted or renamed built-in templates at any time. Icons are grouped by document shape, not unique per template
    • 

  • The "Templates ▾" button in the header toolbar opens the same template list as a quick-access dropdown, grouped by folder exactly like the sidebar (icons included) — useful when the sidebar itself is hidden, e.g. in zen mode. It's read-mostly there: click a template to load it, plus rename/move-to-folder/delete per row, but folders themselves are only created from the sidebar
    • 

  • A line that's exactly "Date:", "Author:", or "Date · Author" in any template (built-in or your own) gets auto-filled the moment the template loads — today's date, and the document's current author if one is set (see Settings → Editing → "Default author" to pre-fill that automatically too). The merged "Date · Author" form fills in with no labels, just the values
    • 

  • Built-in templates use one color convention throughout, worth keeping if you duplicate and extend one: purple = neutral section header, orange = action/decision, red = risk/blocker, green = positive
    • 

  • Want a template in a new doc instead of overwriting? Create a new document first, then load the template
    • 




          


Tabs


    

  • Click a document in the sidebar, or the + at the end of the tab strip → opens it as a tab. Switching tabs keeps each one's undo history, scroll position, and selection independent — it doesn't reset like a plain document switch would
    • 

  • Double-click a tab → rename it
    • 

  • ✕ on a tab, or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + W → closes the active tab only; the document itself isn't deleted and stays in the sidebar. Closing the last tab leaves the editor empty rather than reopening another document
    • 

  • Drag a tab left or right to reorder the strip
    • 

  • ▾ at the right of the tab strip → search all open tabs by name; ↑ / ↓ + Enter to jump, or click a result
    • 

  • Settings → Workspace → "Reopen tabs on launch" controls whether your open tabs come back next time, or you start with just one
    • 




          


Backing up your data


    

  • Everything is stored locally in the browser, scoped to this exact file. Opening a different copy or a newer version of this file starts with empty storage — that's a browser limitation, not a bug
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Auto-backup to file → keeps a live backup written to a file on disk as you work (Chrome/Edge only — Safari and Firefox don't support this yet). Browsers revoke write permission to that file across a reload or restart as a deliberate security measure, so this can need reconnecting — a chip in the status bar flags it when that happens, and clicking it (or "Reconnect" in Settings) restores it in one click. If you disconnect it yourself from Settings, that same chip stays visible with a plain "Connect backup" prompt rather than disappearing, so there's always a way back to it from the status bar. Since the live file always overwrites itself, a "Backup history" list appears underneath once it's written at least once — up to 5 timestamped snapshots, at least 15 minutes apart so a burst of edits doesn't just fill it with near-duplicates, each independently restorable if a bad edit gets written to the live file before you notice
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Local safety copy → a second copy kept automatically in a separate browser storage area; Restore brings it back if the main storage gets cleared. Not a substitute for Export — it's still inside the same browser
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Undo last restore → appears only after you've used Restore (from a backup file, the Local safety copy, or a Backup history entry above) — a snapshot of whatever was in the app right before that restore, in case the file you picked turns out to have been the wrong one. One level deep: it holds only what was there before your most recent restore, not a full history of every restore you've ever done
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Secure Storage → optional, encrypts AI provider keys and the GitHub Gist token at rest (AES-GCM, passphrase-derived key) instead of storing them as plain text — in this browser and in local backup files. The passphrase itself is never stored anywhere; unlocking derives the key fresh each time, and it only lives in memory for that session — reload the page and you'll need to unlock again to use AI features or Gist auto-sync. There's no recovery if you forget it. Turning it off writes everything back as plain text. Once it's set up, a status-bar chip shows the current Locked/Unlocked state and toggles it in one click — no need to open Settings each session; right-click the chip to jump to the full Secure Storage controls instead. Hide it like any other status-bar item in Settings → Statusbar items if you don't want it there. Unlocking also automatically resumes anything that was paused waiting on it — Auto-rewrite on commit and Gist auto-sync each pick back up on their own the moment you unlock, with no need to redo whatever triggered them originally
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Cloud Backup → optional, pushes the same full backup as "Backup all data" to GitHub Gist or Google Drive instead of a local file, so you can pull it down again from a different browser or computer. GitHub Gist needs a Personal Access Token with the gist scope, pasted into Settings — stored in this browser only, the same as an AI provider key (and covered by Secure Storage above, if that's turned on). Google Drive needs your own free OAuth Client ID from Google Cloud Console; the app only ever sees files it created itself, not the rest of your Drive, and the sign-in token lives in memory only — you reconnect each session, nothing Google-related is written to disk. Both providers find your existing backup automatically by name/description on Save and Load, so there's no ID to copy between browsers by hand. Both are manual push/pull by default — Save fully overwrites the cloud copy with what's here, Load fully overwrites what's here with the cloud copy, no merging either direction. Both also offer "Auto-sync on edit" (off by default) — pushes automatically ~1.2s after edits settle, same timing as Auto-backup to file. Gist's auto-sync persists across reloads (the token is saved) and additionally detects conflicts: if the Gist changed elsewhere since your last Save/Load here, auto-push pauses itself and shows a conflict notice rather than silently overwriting the newer version — resolve it with a manual Save or Load. Drive's auto-sync is session-only, since Drive's sign-in token isn't kept across reloads — it turns itself off with a one-time notice (not a nag) whenever the session connection is gone, and needs reconnecting and re-enabling next time; it has no conflict detection of its own, so treat it as a convenience rather than a safety net if you also edit from Drive on another device. Gist's auto-sync also pauses (with a one-time toast, not a nag on every edit) if the token itself can't be read because Secure Storage is locked — nothing is lost in the meantime since local saves and the safety copy never depend on the vault, and the paused push resumes and sends on its own the moment you unlock, no extra edit needed to retrigger it. Neither AI provider keys nor the Gist token — encrypted or not — are ever included in what gets uploaded to Gist or Drive; only your documents are
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Backup / Restore → still the way to move data between files, browsers, or computers, or to take a one-off manual backup; the sidebar footer has Backup/Restore shortcuts too, so you don't need to open Settings just for that. The header's Export ▾ and Import ▾ buttons are for the current document only (Markdown, Word, OPML, etc.) — Backup/Restore cover all your data
    • 

  • Settings → Data → "Warn if this session isn't backed up" → offers Export or Connect auto-backup if you've made changes this session but neither has run yet. Three modes: Strict shows the browser's own "leave site?" prompt when you try to close the tab, then this dialog if you cancel it — a real last-chance gate, at the cost of that generic browser popup (its wording is fixed by the browser, not by Sakura). Passive skips the browser prompt entirely and shows only this dialog, the next time you switch away from or close the tab — quieter, but a direct close with no prior tab-switch gets no warning at all. Off disables both; the status-bar "⚠ Back up your data" chip (from the reminder setting above) is your only remaining signal
    • 

  • Recommended: export before switching to a new version of this file, then import right after opening it
    • 

  • Settings → Data → Reset to default settings → restores appearance/behavior preferences only (theme, toolbar, right-click menu, etc.); your documents and AI settings aren't touched
    • 




          


Note panel


    

  • Every node can hold a rich-text note and, on its own tab, a code block — open the panel via the toolbar ( Note button), right-click → More → Note, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + N (jumps straight to the Note tab; Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K does the same for Code) — clicking the chat-bubble dot that appears on a node with either also opens it directly, defaulting to whichever tab has content (Note first if both do)
    • 

  • Note and Code share one floating, draggable window with a tab switch in its header — one dot on the node row instead of two, and no separate toolbar button or right-click entry for Code. A small </> badge on the dot signals the node also has code, even while you're looking at the Note tab. Drag the header to reposition the panel anywhere; click  to expand to full-screen and click again to restore — maximize applies to whichever tab is active
    • 

  • Note and Code open in a minimal Normal view by default — just the editor, with the toolbar, backlinks, and timestamps tucked away and Rewrite relocated up into the header, since most notes are a short remark rather than something needing the full toolbar. Click  to expand to Maximize — full toolbar, footer, and backlinks/timestamps all visible, Rewrite back in its usual toolbar spot — and click again to restore. Maximize applies to whichever tab is active
    • 

  • Two small badges in the header — a clock and, only when the node is actually referenced elsewhere, a link icon with a count — reveal timestamps or backlinks inline in Normal view without leaving it, so they're reachable without a full Maximize. Both start collapsed each time the panel opens and hide automatically in Maximize, since everything's already showing there. Neither badge appears on the Code tab, which has no equivalent
    • 

  • Toolbar: B I U S for bold / italic / underline / strikethrough;  bullet list and  numbered list;  insert or edit a link, with its own display-text field separate from the URL;  table icon inserts a 2×2 table;  AI Rewrite rewrites the whole note or just selected text; the  summarise button reads the node's child subtree and appends a prose summary to the note
    • 

  • Tab → indents a list item (when inside a list) or inserts spaces in plain text; Shift + Tab → outdent a list item
    • 

  • Highlight any text in the note (Normal or Maximize view) and a small formatting popover appears above it — Bold, Italic, Strikethrough, Highlight (a yellow inline highlight, click again on already-highlighted text to remove it), Link, and Rewrite (AI, rewrites just the highlighted phrase — same underlying call as the toolbar's own AI Rewrite, just pre-scoped to the selection). A quicker route to the same commands as the toolbar above, without moving the cursor up to reach it. Settings → Editing → "Selection formatting popover" turns this off
    • 

  • Rewriting with nothing selected replaces the note's entire content with plain AI-rewritten text, which would otherwise silently remove any images or tables in it — a confirmation prompt catches this and tells you what would be lost before it happens. Select just the text you want rewritten instead if you'd rather keep the rest untouched
    • 

  • Paste images directly into the note — they embed inline and are stored with the note. Click a pasted image to select it, then drag the handle at its corner to resize; double-click it to reset back to its original size
    • 

  • Feature as diagram — right-click any image in a note and choose "Feature as diagram" (e.g. for architecture diagrams) to have it pulled out of the small collapsible note card in Preview and Presenter mode and shown instead as a large, always-visible figure right under the node's text — no click needed to reveal it. The image's alt text (if it has one) shows underneath as a caption. Right-click it again to turn this off and return it to normal note behavior. This also applies to PDF exports, since those render from the same Preview output
    • 

  • Tables: drag the thin border handle between columns to resize column widths
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Z / Ctrl/Cmd + Y undo and redo edits made in this note while it's open — its own undo history, separate from the outline's and separate from the Code tab's, reset each time you open a note and cleared when you close it. The whole session still counts as one step on the outline's own undo when you close the panel. Ctrl/Cmd + Enter or Esc → close and save (whichever tab is active); "Clear note" removes the note text (undoable) and closes the panel
    • 

  • A chat-bubble dot on a node row signals it has a note and/or code — click it to open the panel on its default tab; this also selects that node
    • 

  • Backlinks — if other nodes reference the current node via @mention, a Backlinks section appears at the bottom of the Note tab. Click any entry to jump to that referencing node
    • 

  • Timestamps — the Note tab also shows when the node was created and last modified, on a single line at the bottom. Existing nodes show the time they were first loaded after this feature was added; new nodes and any node whose text is edited going forward will have accurate timestamps
    • 




          


Code (in the Note panel)


    

  • Every node can also hold one code block, on its own Code tab inside the same panel as its Note — there's no separate popup, toolbar button, or right-click entry for it. Switch tabs with the header switch, or jump straight there with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K. A node with code but no note text opens directly to the Code tab when you click its dot
    • 

  • A real <textarea> for editing, syntax-highlighted live as you type — no rich-text formatting, just plain code with color. Line numbers down the left edge. Drag the bottom edge to resize it vertically. Dragging the panel, maximizing it, and closing it all work the same as described above for the Note tab, since it's the same window
    • 

  • Language picker: Plain text, ABAP, SQL, JavaScript, Python, JSON, XML/HTML, Markdown
    • 

  • Copy copies the raw code text (not HTML) to your clipboard
    • 

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Z / Ctrl/Cmd + Y undo and redo edits made in this code block while it's open — its own undo history, separate from the Note tab's, reset each time you open a code block and cleared when you close it. The whole session still counts as one step on the outline's own undo when you close the panel. Ctrl/Cmd + Enter or Esc → close and save; "Clear code" removes it entirely (undoable) and closes the panel
    • 

  • A small </> badge on the node's note dot signals it has a code block, whichever tab you last had open
    • 




          


Decision Log


    

  • A structured decision record — Context, Decision, Rationale, Alternatives, and Impact fields, a Status pill (Proposed/Approved/Rejected) you click to cycle through, and an author/timestamp footer. Lives in its own tab in the Pad, right alongside Notepad, Q&A, and Diagrams — not inserted as an outline node, and each one is a separate record you link to a node rather than something attached directly to it
    • 

  • Click  in the Decision Log tab's toolbar to create one — this is the only way to start a new one, so the tab is always the answer to "where do I add a decision log". If the currently-selected outline node doesn't already have one, it's linked there automatically; otherwise it's created unlinked and the node picker opens right away so you can choose a free node
    • 

  • The tab shows every decision log in the document as a folded row (status pill inline next to the linked node, a one-line snippet of Context or Decision, author/date) — click a row to expand it in place and edit the full card; click it again, or another row, to collapse it. Only one is expanded at a time. Drag the handle that appears on hover to reorder rows manually — same interaction as To-Dos, Diagrams, and Q&A's own row reordering; it's disabled while the "N open" or author filter is narrowing the list, since a filtered view's row order doesn't map cleanly back onto the full list
    • 

  • One decision log per node — the node picker (click the linked-node line inside an expanded card) greys out and explains any node that already has one. Pick "Not linked to a node" to clear the link, freeing that node up
    • 

  • A small accent-colored dot appears next to a node with a linked decision log — click it to jump straight to the Decision Log tab with that entry expanded. The same icon appears next to a folded node's "+N hidden" badge when a decision log is linked somewhere inside its collapsed subtree
    • 

  • A node referenced by a Meeting Notes link or a To-Do's link shows its own small dot too — a calendar icon for meetings, a checklist icon for to-dos — click to jump straight there, or to a small list if more than one links to that same node. Same folded-branch propagation as the decision log dot above. If a meeting or to-do links to the whole document rather than one specific node, look for a small document-shaped badge on that document's tab in the tab strip instead — click it the same way
    • 

  • Delete a decision log from its row (trash icon on hover) — asks for confirmation first (Settings → Editing → "Confirm before delete", same setting used for nodes, Q&A rows, and diagrams), then removes it. Undo with the toast's own Undo button. Deleting doesn't touch the linked node itself
    • 

  • Each field supports light rich text — type "1. " or "- " at the start of a line for a real list, Ctrl/Cmd+B/Ctrl/Cmd+I for bold/italic, no toolbar needed — plus a small AI-rewrite icon that appears on hover
    • 

  • Settings → Panels → Pad → Decision Log: "Ask who decided" prompts for a name each time a new one's created (on by default; the timestamp is always added regardless). "Color field labels" colors the Context/Decision/Rationale/etc. labels — in the editor and in the Decision Log card in Preview — so each field reads at a glance; "Bold field labels" additionally bolds them. Status is separate and always shows as a colored pill either way, since that's a status indicator rather than a fillable field. "Fields to include" hides whichever of the five fields you never fill in, right in the card. "Split large ones onto their own slide" (Presenter/PPTX) gives a decision log with a lot of content its own slide instead of squeezing it under the node's bullets, past a configurable character threshold
    • 

  • Turning Decision Log off (Settings → Features → Pad Panel) hides its tab, node dots, and Excel export — existing decision logs already in the document aren't deleted, just inaccessible until it's turned back on. Clicking a node's decision-log dot while the feature is off shows a reminder of where to re-enable it instead of silently doing nothing
    • 

  • Export menu → Decision Log → Excel (.xlsx) pulls every decision log across the current document into one spreadsheet (timestamp, author, linked node's text, and whichever fields were filled in) — fields left blank export as empty cells; an unlinked decision log's node text exports as blank
    • 




          


Diagrams


    

  • A real draw.io editor, embedded directly — not a static image. Lives in its own tab in the Pad, right alongside Notepad, Q&A, and Decision Log. Each document can hold any number of diagrams
    • 

  • Click  in the Diagrams tab's toolbar to create one — it opens full-screen in draw.io immediately. Click any existing diagram's row in the tab's list to reopen it the same way; the row shows a live thumbnail once it's been saved at least once
    • 

  • Only changes saved inside draw.io itself are kept — its own Save button, or Ctrl/Cmd+S, while the editor is open. Closing the full-screen editor (the × button, or Esc) always asks you to confirm you've saved, since there's no way to force a save from outside draw.io's own UI
    • 

  • Multiple pages/tabs within one diagram: draw.io's own page tabs (bottom-left of the editor) work as usual, and every page's content is kept in full when you save and reopen. But the thumbnail, and the figure shown in Preview/PDF/Word/PowerPoint, can only ever show one page — whichever was active in the editor at the moment you last saved. (An earlier version of this tried exporting every page individually behind the scenes; that turned out to actively corrupt the live diagram — deleting a page from it — so it was reverted. Nothing unsafe is attempted here anymore.) A "N pages" badge appears on a diagram's row in this list as a reminder when this applies. If you want more than one page to actually appear in the document, create them as separate diagrams (the + button) and link each to its own node instead of using pages within one diagram
    • 

  • Give it a title in the field at the top, and link it to a node with the anchor button next to it ("Not linked to a node" clears the link). Linking is optional but is what makes a diagram show up anywhere outside this tab — an unlinked diagram only lives in the Diagrams list itself. The node link can also be changed right from a diagram's row in the list — click the "Under: …" (or "Not linked to a node") line, same picker either way — without needing to open the full-screen editor first
    • 

  • A linked diagram appears as a large figure directly under its node in Preview, PDF, Word, and PowerPoint exports — same treatment as a note image marked "Feature as diagram" elsewhere in Sakura, so there's no visible difference between the two workflows in the output. Settings → Export & print → "Include Diagrams in exports" controls the Word/PowerPoint case specifically (Preview and PDF always show it); click the figure in Preview to enlarge it
    • 

  • A small accent-colored square-cluster dot appears next to a node with a linked diagram — click it to jump straight to the Diagrams tab and open that diagram (or the list, if the node has more than one). The same icon appears next to a folded node's "+N hidden" badge when a diagram is linked somewhere inside its collapsed subtree
    • 

  • Delete a diagram from its row in the list (trash icon on hover) — asks for confirmation first (Settings → Editing → "Confirm before delete", same setting used for nodes and Q&A rows), then removes it and its node link entirely. Undo with Ctrl/Cmd+Z or the toast's own Undo button, either works, for 5 seconds — same window as every other delete-undo toast in Sakura. Deleting doesn't touch the node itself
    • 

  • The copy icon next to delete duplicates a diagram — same drawing, same status, same node link, titled "(original title) (copy)". Useful for variations on the same base drawing without starting over. A pure data copy, not a draw.io operation, so it's instant
    • 

  • Each diagram has a status — Draft, In Progress, Review, or Final — shown as a small colored pill on its row and in the full-screen editor's header. Click either one to cycle to the next status. Purely organizational — nothing else in Sakura reads it (it's not exported or shown in Preview), it's just a way to track where each diagram stands at a glance across a document with several
    • 

  • Two more chips can appear on a row: Unlinked, when a diagram has no node link yet (a reminder it's invisible in Preview and exports until you link it), and Modified since export, when it's changed since the last time anything was exported from Sakura. The latter is approximate — Sakura tracks one "last export" timestamp app-wide, not per document, so it can't tell whether it was specifically this diagram (or even this document) that got exported
    • 

  • Each diagram has a private, one-line note — a reminder to yourself, e.g. "needs sign-off before this is final." Type directly into the small field under a row (or the matching one in the full-screen editor's header) — no popup, no separate edit step. Enter or clicking away saves it, Esc discards whatever was typed since it was last saved. Never shown in Preview, PDF, Word, PowerPoint, or anywhere outside this panel
    • 

  • Diagrams, Q&A, and Pad content are all included in Sakura's Version History now (More menu → Version History for the open document, or right-click a document in the sidebar → Version history) — restoring an older version restores all three alongside the outline text. A restored diagram's thumbnail is blank until it's reopened and saved once in draw.io, which regenerates it automatically; the diagram's actual content isn't affected, only the cached preview image. Pad content restores as text only — any file attachments or pasted images that were in the Pad at that point in time are gone for good, replaced with a small note of what was there, since keeping every version of a 5 MB attachment would make History unusably large. Restoring a version with older revisions recorded before this existed leaves diagrams/Q&A/Pad exactly as they currently are, rather than guessing or wiping them — but if one of those diagrams or decision logs is anchored to a node that only exists in the newer state you're restoring away from, it becomes unlinked from any node once the outline rolls back (the data itself isn't deleted — it still shows up in the Diagrams tab or Decision Log list, just without a node to hang off — the same "not linked to a node" state either already shows for a deliberately-detached one). The "Version restored" toast names how many diagrams/decision logs this happened to, if any, so it's never silent
    • 

  • Open or close the tab with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + G, same "press again to close" behavior as the other Pad tabs' shortcuts
    • 

  • Turning Diagrams off (Settings → Features → Pad Panel) hides its tab, toolbar button, node dots, and keyboard shortcut — existing diagrams already in the document aren't deleted, just inaccessible (and left out of Preview/exports) until it's turned back on
    • 

  •  Import — brings in an existing .drawio/.xml file: drag one onto the dropzone, or click it to browse. "Paste XML text instead" is there too for XML that's only sitting in your clipboard rather than saved as a file. Either way it's added to this list, linked to the selected node if it's free, with its thumbnail generated automatically. A small "Copy a prompt built from your outline" link is included for anyone who wants an AI assistant's help getting started — paste the prompt into whichever one you like, save or copy its reply, then bring that back in through Import
    • 




          


Pad


    

  • A docked panel for whole-document notes — separate from per-node Notes above. One Pad per document, not tied to any single node
    • 

  • Open it with the small panel icon docked at the bottom-right of the editor, next to the maximize button, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P; the same shortcut and button close it again. It stays open if the editor is maximized, and that floating button is how you open or close it while maximized too — handy if Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar → "Show toolbar" is off and the regular toolbar isn't around to use instead
    • 

  • Drag the thin strip on its left edge to resize it; width is remembered, as is whether it's open or closed
    • 

  • Settings → Pad → "Notepad" toggle hides the panel and its floating toggle button entirely if you don't use it (on by default)
    • 

  • The Pad's header bar (Notepad / Q&A / Decision Log / Diagrams tabs, plus the close button) is always there now — it's how you switch modes, so it's no longer an optional toggle. "Match editor background" controls whether Pad's writing surface looks like the outline's canvas or reads as visually distinct chrome — personal preference, no right answer. Settings → Pad → "Default pad view" picks which of the four tabs greets you every time you open the Pad — you can still switch between them with these same header tabs while it's open
    • 

  • Settings → Pad → "Toolbar buttons" lets you hide individual buttons (Bold, Table, AI Rewrite, etc.) you don't use
    • 

  • Toolbar mirrors the Note panel: B I U S,  bullet /  numbered lists,  link (with its own display-text field, separate from the URL),  table insert with resizable columns,  image paste/insert (click to select, drag the corner handle to resize, double-click to reset), and  AI Rewrite (rewrites the selection, or the whole Pad if nothing's selected). No subtree-summarise here — there's no subtree at the document level
    • 

  • File attachments — click the  paperclip toolbar button and pick any file, drag a file straight onto the Pad, or paste one you've copied from your file manager — any of the three inserts a small chip (with a file-type icon — PDF, image, spreadsheet, doc, slides, archive, audio, video, or code, based on extension) showing the filename and size, which stays inline with your notes. Click the chip to download that file again; hover it for a remove button. Each file is capped at 5 MB, since it's stored as part of the Pad's own saved content (same as pasted images) rather than uploaded anywhere — which also means it's included automatically in every existing backup path (local safety copy, auto-backup-to-file, export/import), with no separate attachment backup to think about
    • 

  • Running AI Rewrite with nothing selected replaces the Pad's entire content with plain rewritten text — which would silently remove any attachments, images, or tables it contains. A confirmation prompt now catches this case and tells you what would be lost before it happens; select just the text you want rewritten instead if you'd rather keep everything else untouched
    • 

  • Highlight any text on the Notepad tab and a small formatting popover appears above it — Bold, Italic, Strikethrough, Highlight (a yellow inline highlight, click again on already-highlighted text to remove it), Link, and Rewrite (AI, rewrites just the highlighted phrase — same underlying call as the toolbar's own AI Rewrite, just pre-scoped to the selection, with the same confirmation logic not applying since a selection is already guaranteed here). Settings → Editing → "Selection formatting popover" turns this off
    • 

  • Pad has its own undo history, separate from the outline's — Ctrl/Cmd + Z / Ctrl/Cmd + Y undo and redo typing, AI Rewrite, and "Clear pad" for the current document's Pad. It never appears on the outline's own Ctrl/Cmd+Z stack, since Pad content isn't part of that snapshot — the two undo histories are independent, and Pad's is per-document, cleared on reload the same way the outline's is. For anything further back than that session's undo history, Version History (More menu → Version History) also keeps periodic snapshots of Pad text — restoring one brings back Pad's text (not file attachments or pasted images, which aren't kept in History)
    • 




          


To-Dos


    

  • An app-level task list — not tied to any document, node, or Pad. One shared list across your whole workspace
    • 

  • Open it from its button at the end of the status bar with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + U; the same shortcut closes it again
    • 

  • Floating and draggable by its header; drag the thin strip on its left edge to resize its width (remembered between sessions, same as its position).
