The  button expands it full-screen the same way Note/Pad/Code do (temporarily overriding your custom width, restored when you leave full-screen); the  button next to it exports the whole list as plain text
    • 

  • Type a task and press Enter (or use the + button) to add it — the box grows as you type so long tasks stay readable. Shift+Enter inserts a line break instead of adding the task, both here and while editing an existing task's text
    • 

  • The clock icon next to Export opens Version History for the whole list — same idea as a document's Version History, kept separately since To-Dos isn't tied to any document. A version is saved automatically roughly every 10 minutes a change is made, or click "Save a version now" right before something risky. Restoring one replaces the current list (after saving a checkpoint of what was there first, so restoring is never itself destructive) — keeps the last 20 versions, app-wide
    • 

  • Checking a task off moves it straight to Completed, most-recently-finished first; unchecking sends it back to Open
    • 

  • The Open row shows how many open tasks match the current filter, with a magnifying-glass quick-find icon, sort  and filter  icons at its right edge. Sort covers Manual order, Priority, and Due date; filter narrows the list to one priority level (or tasks with none). Any icon turns accent-colored whenever it's off its default (or quick-find is open) so you can tell at a glance that something's active
    • 

  • Click the magnifying-glass icon to open quick-find and type to search task text, across both Open and Completed. Matches are highlighted and non-matching tasks are hidden entirely (not just dimmed), so the list actually shortens as you narrow it down — this composes with the priority filter, so a priority filter plus a search term shows only tasks matching both. Esc clears the search text first, then a second Esc (or clicking the icon again) closes it; the search always resets when you close the panel. Dragging to reorder still works correctly while filtered — a task dropped next to a visible neighbor lands there in the full list too, even if other hidden tasks sit between them. This searches task text only, live, within the panel — for a version that also opens the panel and jumps to the match from anywhere in the app, Quick Assist's todo: scope still does that
    • 

  • Drag the handle that appears on hover to reorder open tasks. Completed tasks aren't draggable — they're always ordered by completion time
    • 

  • Editing a task's text supports Ctrl/Cmd + Z / Ctrl/Cmd + Y to undo/redo that text edit — its own history, shared across all tasks (app-level, like the list itself) rather than per-task. This covers text edits only: adding, deleting, reordering, and completing tasks keep their existing in-panel Undo toasts instead
    • 

  • Hover a task for a spark  AI Rewrite button and a delete button — both sit as an overlay rather than reserving space, so task text keeps the full row width when you're not hovering. Deleting shows an "Undo" toast, inside the panel itself, for a few seconds in case of a misclick — past that window, Version History (the clock icon) can still bring back a deleted task as part of restoring an earlier snapshot of the whole list
    • 

  • The Completed section header shows a Clear completed button whenever it has tasks (respecting the current priority filter) to remove them all in one action, with its own in-panel Undo toast — a batch counterpart to deleting one at a time. All To-Dos toasts (Undo prompts, export confirmation, AI rewrite errors) show inside the panel while it's open rather than the app's global toast, so acting on one never closes the panel
    • 

  • An overdue count badge can appear on the To-Dos button itself (app bar or status bar) whenever you have open, overdue tasks — visible without opening the panel. Toggle it off at Settings → To-Dos → "Overdue count badge" if you'd rather not see it
    • 

  • Priority, due date, and link chips sit below the task text. Priority cycles None → Low → Medium → High on click; due date shows "Today/Tomorrow" or the date, turning red once it's overdue. All three stay visible once set, and only reveal on hover while unset. Clicking the due date chip opens a small calendar popover with Today / Tomorrow / Next week presets up top for the common cases, month navigation for picking any other date, and a "Clear due date" option. Clicking the link chip opens a small popover with two tabs — Web link (an optional display-text field, a URL field, and Save; a bare domain or path is treated as an https:// link automatically) and Link to a node (a search box over your active documents — the same lookup Quick Assist and Global Search use — with results grouped into Documents, for linking the whole document, and Nodes, for one specific spot in it; leave the box empty to browse and pick a document without typing its title first), plus a Remove link option that clears whichever kind is set. A web-link chip shows your display text if you set one, or the link's hostname otherwise; a document-link chip shows the document's title with a plain document icon, and a node-link chip shows that node's own text with a small hierarchy icon, in place of the link-chain icon, so either reads as "jumps within Sakura" rather than "opens a website" — and both stay live, tracking the current title/text rather than a snapshot. Once any kind of link is attached, an open/jump button appears in the row's hover actions, next to Rewrite and Delete: for a web link it opens a new tab; for a document or node link it switches to that document if needed (scrolling to and briefly flashing the node, the same way backlinks do, when it's a node link specifically). If the node or document has since been deleted, the chip is shown with a dashed outline and clicking either it or the jump button gives a "That node no longer exists" (or "That document no longer exists") toast instead of failing silently
    • 

  • A task promoted from a Meeting Notes action item shows a small calendar-icon "from meeting" chip instead — separate from the link chip above, since a task can have both at once. Click it to jump back to the source meeting note; a small × on it removes just the reference, not the task. If that meeting note was later deleted, the chip switches to a dashed "missing" style rather than failing silently
    • 

  • A task can either repeat or hold sub-tasks — never both at once. Click the Repeat chip to cycle None → Daily → Weekdays → Weekly; checking off a recurring task doesn't complete it, it just advances the due date to the next occurrence (a toast confirms the new date), so recurring tasks never pile up in Completed. Once a repeat is set, a small repeat glyph also shows on the due-date chip, even before you've set a due date, so it's clear at a glance that a task recurs
    • 

  • Hover a task and click Subtask to open an inline field — Enter adds it (Escape cancels), building a small nested checklist under the task with its own checkboxes, an n/300 character counter while typing, and a delete button per item on hover. A progress badge (e.g. "2/4") plus a collapse arrow appear once a task has any sub-tasks; the parent task's own checkbox is disabled and completes automatically the moment every sub-task is checked — you can't check the parent by hand while it has sub-tasks. Setting a repeat on a task removes its sub-tasks' ability to be added (and vice versa, adding a sub-task clears any repeat)
    • 

  • Right-click a task for a context menu — mark complete/open, duplicate the task, copy its text, move it to the top or bottom of the list, expand/collapse its sub-tasks, or delete it. Right-clicking directly on the task's own text, or on a sub-task row, falls through to the normal browser menu instead, so copy/paste and spellcheck still work there
    • 

  • Settings → To-Dos: dock the toggle button in the status bar instead of the app bar; show or hide the overdue count badge; auto-expire completed tasks after a set number of days; cap how many completed tasks are kept (oldest drop first); choose whether row actions (drag handle, priority/due/repeat/link chips, Rewrite/Subtask/Delete) stay always visible or only reveal on hover; and whether a task's first sub-task list starts collapsed by default instead of expanded
    • 

  • Typing @ directly in a task's text renders an inline chip: @name for an assignee, @date (ISO date, @today/@tomorrow, or a weekday like @fri) for a date reference, and @status (waiting/blocked/done/review) for status. A suggestion popup appears as you type, offering the date/status keywords plus any names you've already used as @name elsewhere — Arrow keys to navigate, Enter/Tab to accept, Escape to dismiss. These are colored text only — display convenience within the task text, not linked to the priority/due-date/link fields above
    • 

  • #tag renders as a colored tag chip — same autocomplete popup, suggesting tags you've already used. >remark renders everything from the > to the end of the line as a muted italic aside. Both are display only, same as @ tokens
    • 

  • Quick-find already searches the raw task text, so it matches #tags, @mentions, and remark text without any special handling — priority stays a separate structured field with its own dedicated filter
    • 

  • Quick Assist searches your tasks too — type a task's text, or scope it with todo: milk — and clicking a result opens the panel and flashes the match
    • 




          


Meeting Notes


    

  • An app-level meeting log — not tied to any document. One shared list across your whole workspace, same idea as To-Dos
    • 

  • Open it from its button at the end of the status bar with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + E; the same shortcut closes it again
    • 

  • Click  to add a blank meeting note, or the layout icon next to it for New from template — Meeting Notes, 1:1, Daily Standup, Weekly Status, Project Kickoff, or Retrospective, each pre-filling the title/Agenda/Notes fields (and a starter action item, for the templates that call for one) so you're not starting from nothing. Either way it opens expanded and ready to type into immediately. Notes are listed newest first, click any row to expand it in place and edit it, click again (or click another row) to fold it back up
    • 

  • Each note has a title, date, and an optional time of day (shown on the row next to the date, e.g. "2:30 PM") — just a quick time reference, not a full scheduling system, so there's no separate end time or duration to fill in. Below that: attendee chips (type a name, press Enter or , to add one; Backspace on an empty field removes the last), an Agenda field and a Notes field (both light rich text — type "1. " or "- " at the start of a line for a real list, or use the bullet/numbered buttons next to each field's label; Ctrl/Cmd+B/Ctrl/Cmd+I for bold/italic, with their own undo/redo, plus a small AI-rewrite icon that appears on hover next to each field's label). Highlight any text in either field and a small formatting popover appears above it — Bold, Italic, Strikethrough, Highlight (a yellow inline highlight, click again on already-highlighted text to remove it), Link, and Rewrite (AI, rewrites just the highlighted phrase); Link and Rewrite have no other way in from these two fields' own fixed toolbar. Settings → Editing → "Selection formatting popover" turns this off if it's not wanted. Ctrl/Cmd + Space in the title, attendees, Agenda, Notes, or any action item field opens a small character-insert menu (em dash, en dash, arrow, check mark, cross mark, middle dot, date/time) — the same mini menu Node Quick Assist uses elsewhere, restricted to what makes sense outside outline editing. Below the fields sits a list of action items
    • 

  • Action items are simple checkboxes with editable text. The Promote icon next to one adds it as a real To-Do — the task text stays clean; instead, the meeting's date (if set) becomes the new task's due date, and a small "from meeting" chip appears on the To-Do row linking back to this note (click it to jump straight there; a small × removes just the link, not the task). Once promoted the icon turns into a checkmark (green) and can't be promoted again, so re-clicking never creates a duplicate
    • 

  • Linked nodes: click the link icon to search — results are grouped into Documents (matched by title) and Nodes (matched by text, with a highlighted snippet), same layout as To-Dos' own link search. Leave the search box empty to browse and pick a whole document without typing its title first. A node-link chip shows a small node icon, a document-link chip a document icon, so either reads as "jumps within Sakura" at a glance. Click an attached link to jump straight there; if the node or document has since been deleted, it's shown with a warning color and clicking it explains why instead of failing silently
    • 

  • Export a single meeting note as formatted text (download icon on hover, next to trash) — a .md file using Markdown syntax (headings, **bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, bullets, links) carried over from what you typed, not flattened to bare plain text. The title and section labels (Agenda, Notes, Action Items, Linked Nodes) use the smallest Markdown heading level (H6 — "Subsection" in Windows 11 Notepad's own heading dropdown), so they still read as real headings rather than plain body text, without the oversized look a top-level heading gets in Notepad's formatted view. Genuine headings you actually typed inside Agenda/Notes are kept as real Markdown headings too, nudged down toward that same small end of the scale rather than reproduced at their original (often much larger) level. Windows 11's modern Notepad and other Markdown-aware apps render this as real formatting — Notepad specifically only keeps that rendering on save if the file is named .md, so that's the extension used here rather than .txt. Action items come through as a checklist (completed ones struck through), and linked nodes/documents as a plain list. To-Dos' own list export and Q&A's own export use the same approach
    • 

  • Delete a note from its row (trash icon on hover) — with an in-panel "Undo" toast, same pattern as To-Dos and the document-level panels
    • 

  • The clock icon in the panel header opens Version history for the whole list — same idea as To-Dos' own, kept separately since Meeting Notes isn't tied to any document. A version is saved automatically roughly every 10 minutes a change is made, or click "Save a version now" right before something risky. Restoring one replaces the current list (after saving a checkpoint of what was there first) — keeps the last 20 versions, app-wide
    • 

  • Settings → Meeting Notes: dock the toggle button in the status bar instead of the app bar, same as To-Dos
    • 




          


Feature activation


    

  • Settings →  Features — on/off switches for the handful of capabilities most people actually turn off (Note, Code, Tagging, AI, Pad, To-Dos, Quick Assist). Panel-specific extras like Decision Log, Focus, Backlinks, Hover Toolbar, and Node Quick Assist have their own enable switch inside that panel's own Settings section instead — see each one's Help entry below for details. A small dot appears next to "Features" in the rail when any capability here is off, as a reminder
    • 

  • Note Panel — per-node rich-text notes. Off: the note toolbar button, right-click action, note dot on nodes, and the note panel itself are all hidden. Existing note content is preserved — turning it back on restores everything
    • 

  • Pad Panel — a docked whole-document notepad, separate from per-node notes. Off: the floating Pad toggle button (bottom-right of the editor) and the Settings → Pad section are hidden. The Pad's content is preserved
    • 

  • To-Dos — the app-level task list, not tied to any document. Off: the app bar/status bar To-Dos button, its panel, and the Settings → To-Dos section are hidden. Existing tasks are preserved
    • 

  • AI Capabilities — the Rewrite, Expand, Summarise, Generate outline, Suggest icon, Suggest tags, and Restructure text (Import menu) features. Off: the AI toolbar group, all matching right-click items, the "Restructure text" entry in Import ▾, and every section under Settings → AI are hidden. Word import (Import ▾ → Word) still works while this is off, but loses its automatic AI fallback for documents that have no heading styles to extract — those import as a flat list instead. Requires a provider API key (Settings → AI → Provider) before anything will run — select a provider and the key hint shows where to get one
    • 

  • Tagging — #tag chips on nodes. Off: the tag toolbar button, right-click action, and tag chips on all nodes are hidden. Tags are still stored in the document data; turning the feature back on restores them
    • 

  • Quick Assist — the search box in the status bar (Ctrl/Cmd+Space), which handles both plain-English commands and general search in one place. Off: the shortcut does nothing, the box disappears from the status bar, and any open dropdown closes immediately. Search-result behavior inside it (on/off, category filter, result cap) has its own dedicated section at Settings → Workspace → Quick Assist
    • 

  • Turning a feature off hides every entry point for it (toolbar, right-click, Settings sub-section, Help sub-section). The underlying data is never deleted — toggling a feature is non-destructive in both directions. Feature states are saved alongside your other preferences and restored on the next launch
    • 




          
Start with structure. Refine later.

Product launch plan

              

                
                
                
              

            

            

              
Launch checklist

              
Marketing

              
•Write & review landing page copy with marketing

              

                
Note

                Need 3 variants — square, story, banner
              

              
•Design social graphics

              
Engineering

              

                
Decision: Use Stripe over Braintree — lower fees, better docs

              🌸🌸🌸
              🌸🌸🌸
            

            

              

              
🌸

              
Sakura in action

              

              
Like a cherry blossom tree, every idea here grows as a branch — nested, structured, beautiful.

              
We're going to build out a real document: a "Product launch plan" outline with Marketing and Engineering branches. Along the way you'll see formatting, an AI rewrite, a note, a decision log, a quick Pad reminder, then a preview and a Word export — all on that one plan. Press Start when you're ready.

              
            

          

          
          

            

              

              
🌸

              
The design philosophy behind Sakura

              
Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication.

              
— Leonardo da Vinci

              
Start your own — every idea grows as a branch.

            

          

        

      

      

    

    

  

  

    

      

      

        
        
        
        
        
        
        
        
      

    

    

    

      

      

      

    

    

      S A K U R A
      0:00
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
    

    

    

    

      
NotepadSaved to this document's Pad

      

      

    

    

    
Laser pointer✓

    

  

  

  

    

      

        🌸
        Sakura
      

      
Welcome — where would you like to start?

      
Pick how you want to get familiar. You can always revisit both from the Help menu.

      

        
        
      

      
      
      
    

  


  

    

      
🌸Sakura

      
Why an outliner — and why Sakura?

      
Think of an outliner as turning your notes into a family tree, instead of a messy pile of paragraphs. Instead of writing left to right, you write in points that tuck inside one another. Here's why that makes life easier:

      

        

          
          

            See the big picture without the clutter
            Five pages of notes usually means scrolling forever to find what you want. In Sakura, you can collapse the details and see just your main headings — like a table of contents. Click one open when you need it.
          

        

        

          
          

            Move things around like LEGO blocks
            In a normal document, moving a section means highlighting, cutting, pasting, and fixing the spacing. Here you just grab one point and drag it — everything tucked underneath comes with it automatically.
          

        

        

          
          

            Bring in the mess, get back structure
            Paste in messy notes or import a Word document — Sakura sorts it into a tree for you, AI-assisted when it needs to be. This part's specific to Sakura, not outliners in general.
          

        

        

          
          

            It keeps you thinking, not just typing
            Plain note-taking lets you write down what someone said without really processing it. An outliner makes you decide, in the moment: is this a new idea, or a detail under the last one? That keeps you actively sorting instead of just transcribing.
          

        

      

      
Not every kind of writing wants this. Flowing essays and loosely connected notes are still better off elsewhere — Sakura's built for things that have a shape: plans, specs, architecture, meeting notes, decisions.

      
    

  


  

    

      
Section icon library

      
These are the only emoji that get swapped for a matching SVG line icon on section-header rows. Anything not shown here stays as plain emoji — that's the mix "Render section icons as SVG" warns about.

      

      
    

  


  

    

      
Scan outline for questions

      
Every node ending in "?" — uncheck any you don't want. Only your outline selection starts checked if you had question nodes selected.

      

      

        
        
      

    

  


  

    

      
Import Q&A

      
Paste text below — Q: / A: pairs (this also reads Sakura's own Q&A export format back in), or just a list of questions ending in "?" if there's nothing else to go on.

      
      

        
        
      

    

  


  

    

      

      

      

      

      

      

      

    

  

  

    

      

        Version History — 
        
      

      

      

        Keeps the last 20 versions per document
        
      

    

  

  

    

      

        To-Dos Version History
        
      

      

      

        Keeps the last 20 versions, app-wide (not tied to any document)
        
      

    

  

  

    

      

        Meeting Notes Version History
        
      

      

      

        Keeps the last 20 versions, app-wide (not tied to any document)
        
      

    

  



  

    
    
  

  

  
No tags yet





  

    

      
Toolbar Buttons
Choose which icon groups and individual buttons appear on the main toolbar

      
    

    

      

        
        

          Hide groups you don't use — keyboard-first workflows often skip History or Structure
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole History section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep one and drop the other
          

          

          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Structure section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Structure buttons and drop others
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Move section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep one and drop the other
          

          

          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Format section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Format buttons and drop others
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Fold section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Fold buttons and drop others
          

          

          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Insert section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Insert buttons and drop others, e.g. keep Note & Checkbox but hide Date/Time & Tags
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole AI section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some AI buttons and drop others
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Same effect as hiding the whole Delete section above — included here for consistency with the other groups
          

          

          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Hiding the whole Extras section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Extras actions and drop others (applies to both the Extras menu and the buttons shown when Expanded is on)
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

    

  





  

    

      
Right-click Menu
Choose which actions appear when you right-click a node

      
    

    

      

        
        

          Pick which actions show as icons at the top of a node's right-click menu — everything else stays one click away under "More". Sort children always stays under More since it's a group, not a single action.
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

      

        
        

          Turn off an action here to remove it from the right-click menu entirely — quick row and "More" both. Unlike "Quick row" above, this isn't about placement, it's full removal.
          

          

          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        

        

      

    

  








  

    

      
Keyboard Shortcuts
Click a combo to change it · Esc cancels · needs Ctrl/Cmd or Alt

      
    

    

      

        
        
        
      

      
    

    

      

      

        

        
No matching shortcuts

        

      

    

    
Arrow keys, Tab, Enter/F2, and Delete are core tree navigation and aren't customizable here.

  





  

    

      
Sakura Cheatsheet
Everything at a glance — full detail lives in Help

      
    

    


      

        
        
        
      

      

        

        

      
No matching shortcuts


      

      

        
The Basics

        
    
          
  • Enter/F2 edit node
    • 
          
  • Enter new sibling (while editing)
    • 
          
  • Shift+Enter split at cursor
    • 
          
  •  new child
    • 
          
  • Tab/Shift+Tab indent / outdent
    • 
          
  • / move node
    • 
          
  • ←/→ collapse / expand
    • 
          
  • Click select · Shift+click range · Ctrl/Cmd+click multi
    • 
          
  • Right-click → icon row (Zoom in, Note, Tags, Rewrite, Delete by default) + More for move, sort, and everything else; customisable in Settings → Right-click menu
    • 
        

      


      

        
Semantics

        
    
          
  • [Text] → Section label
    • 
          
  • (Text) → note muted italic
    • 
          
  • !Text → alert red warning
    • 
          
  • `Text` → code inline code
    • 
          
  • >Text → quoted content — attribution, citation, direct quote
    • 
          
  • @Node name → clickable backlink (type @ while editing to pick)
    • 
          
  • [ ] text or [x] text → auto-converts to checkbox on commit
    • 
          
  • Outline numbering — Settings → Editing → "Outline numbering" (1, 1.1, 1.1.1)
    • 
        

      


      

        
Formatting

        
    
          
  •  bold
    • 
          
  •  italic
    • 
          
  •  underline
    • 
          
  •  strike
    • 
          
  •  highlight
    • 
          
  •  text color
    • 
          
  • –6 heading 1–6 (independent of depth)
    • 
          
  • [Text] → Section · (Text) → note
    • 
          
  • !Text → alert · `Text` → code
    • 
          
  • @Node name → backlink (type @ while editing to pick)
    • 
          
  • [ ] or [x] at start → auto-converts to checkbox on commit
    • 
        

      


      

        
Toolbar

        
    
          
  • Undo / Redo —  / 
    • 
          
  • Add child — 
    • 
          
  • Select all — 
    • 
          
  • B I U S, highlight, color — text styling
    • 
          
  • Collapse / Expand all —  or ▶ button
    • 
          
  •  Intelligent paste — parses indents/bullets into nodes
    • 
          
  •  Insert Date/Time — 
    • 
          
  •  Note — 
    • 
        

      


      

        
Search & Quick Assist

        
    
          
  •  Quick Assist — plain-English commands, or search anything
    • 
          
  • While editing a node, the same shortcut opens a smaller node-specific menu instead (em dash, date/time, note, code block, tags, rewrite) — see Node Quick Assist below
    • 
          
  • Covers settings, help, doc & template names, node text, notes, code blocks, tags, and the pad — across all documents
    • 
          
  • Results group by type ("In documents", "Notes", "Code", "Tags", "Pad") with a snippet — click to jump straight there
    • 
          
  •  search this tree
    • 
          
  •  search & replace
    • 
          
  • Sidebar filter only matches doc/template names — use header search for text inside docs
    • 
        

      


      

        
Pad & Notes

        
    
          
  • Note — per-node, rich text · 
    • 
          
  • Floating, draggable,  to expand full-screen
    • 
          
  • Pad — one per document, not tied to a node · 
    • 
          
  • Both: B I U S, lists, link, table, image paste,  AI rewrite
    • 
          
  • Pad only: attach a file (button, drag, or paste) — up to 5 MB, shown as a downloadable chip; stored inline so it's covered by every existing backup path
    • 
          
  • Note adds a subtree-summarise button; Pad doesn't (no subtree)
    • 
          
  • "Clear pad" isn't on the undo stack — per-node note clear is
    • 
          
  • Note panel shows Backlinks — nodes that @mention this one (click to jump)
    • 
          
  • Note panel shows Created / Modified timestamps for the node
    • 
        

      


      

        
To-Dos

        
    
          
  • App-level task list — not tied to any document or node · 
    • 
          
  • Floating, draggable,  to expand full-screen
    • 
          
  • Check off a task to move it to Completed (newest first); drag the hover handle to reorder Open tasks
    • 
          
  • Priority and due-date chips below each task; both auto-hide until set or hovered. A task promoted from a Meeting Notes action item gets a "from meeting" chip too — click to jump back to the note
    • 
          
  • A task can repeat (Daily/Weekdays/Weekly — completing it just advances the due date) or hold sub-tasks (nested checklist, parent auto-completes when all are checked) — not both at once
    • 
          
  • Right-click a task for a context menu — duplicate, copy text, move to top/bottom, expand/collapse sub-tasks, delete
    • 
          
  • Type @ in task text for inline chips, with autocomplete — @name assignee, @date (e.g. @tomorrow, @fri), @status (waiting/blocked/done/review) — display only
    • 
          
  • # for a #tag (autocompletes from prior tags), > for a muted remark to end of line — both display only
    • 
          
  • Quick-find matches #tags, @mentions, and remark text too — it's a plain substring search over the task text
    • 
          
  • Filter by priority, status, and due date at once (Overdue/Today/Due later/No due date) — all three combine, and the filter menu stays open so you can set several before closing it
    • 
          
  • Select multiple open tasks (checklist icon) to bulk-set priority, status, or due date, or bulk-complete/delete — each with its own Undo, same as the single-task actions
    • 
          
  • AI (each independently toggleable in Settings → AI → To-Dos AI): header buttons to extract action items from the current document/selection, or generate a status summary of open tasks; a per-task button to break it into 3-6 subtasks — all add directly with Undo, same as Rewrite on each task
    • 
          
  • Settings → To-Dos: app bar or status bar placement, auto-expiry, cap on completed tasks, hover vs. always-visible row actions, sub-tasks collapsed by default
    • 
          
  • Searchable from Quick Assist — plain text or todo: milk
    • 
        

      


      

        
Meeting Notes

        
    
          
  • App-level, like To-Dos — not tied to any document or node · 
    • 
          
  • Chronological list, newest first — click a row to expand it in place; title, date, attendees, agenda, notes, action items, and links to any node in any document
    • 
          
  • Action items have a Promote button that adds them as a real To-Do — sets the new task's due date from the meeting's date and links back with a "from meeting" chip, instead of touching the task text — a second click is disabled once it's already been promoted
    • 
          
  • Attendees: type a name and press Enter or , to add a chip; Backspace on an empty field removes the last one
    • 
        

      


      

        
Code (Note panel)

        
    
          
  • A tab in the Note panel, not a separate popup — one per node, live syntax-highlighted <textarea> editing with line numbers, no rich text · 
    • 
          
  • 8 languages: Plain text, ABAP, SQL, JavaScript, Python, JSON, XML/HTML, Markdown
    • 
          
  • Shares the Note panel's window — same drag, maximize, and node dot (with a small </> badge)
    • 
          
  • Copy button copies raw code text, not HTML
    • 
        

      


      

        
AI Features

        
    
          
  •  Rewrite — selected node(s), or highlighted text
    • 
          
  • Right-click → More → Rewrite document for the whole tree
    • 
          
  •  Generate outline · 
    • 
          
  • Restructure text — Import menu → paste messy/unstructured text → organized into nodes, with wording cleaned up as needed to read well as an outline (won't invent facts not in the source); always opens as a new document, original text kept in its Pad for reference; already-structured pastes (bullets/indents) skip the AI call · 
    • 
          
  • Word import — Import menu → .docx file: uses the file's own heading/list styles directly, no AI needed when they're present; falls back to AI restructuring only when the document has no heading styles at all. Tables become a node per row with cells as children; images become a "[image]" placeholder. Always opens as a new document
    • 
          
  •  Suggest icon — picks a fitting emoji prefix
    • 
          
  • Batches over 30 nodes send in sequential chunks
    • 
          
  • Expand node — AI toolbar → expand button (single node selected) → breaks into child nodes
    • 
          
  • Suggest tags — AI toolbar → tag button → applies relevant tags from existing doc tags or new ones
    • 
          
  • Summarise selection — select 2+ nodes → AI toolbar → summarise button → inserts a parent label above them
    • 
          
  • Settings → AI → Provider: choose from six built-in providers (Gemini, Groq, Claude, OpenRouter, Cerebras, GitHub Models) or add your own — paste your API key; all AI features share the same active provider
    • 
        

      


      

        
Export

        
    
          
  •  copy as text
    • 
          
  •  copy selection as image
    • 
          
  • Markdown .md
    • 
          
  • Tree .txt
    • 
          
  • PDF .pdf — prints Preview/Presenter's actual rendered output, follows its fold state
    • 
          
  • PowerPoint .pptx — real editable slides + bullets, diagram images embedded, follows Preview's fold state
    • 
          
  • Word .docx — real Heading styles + updatable TOC, Decision Log cards, checkboxes, rich-formatted notes, diagram images, optional cover page, follows Preview's fold state
    • 
          
  • Decision Log → Excel .xlsx
    • 
          
  • Export ▾ / Import ▾ in header — document-level (Markdown, Word, OPML…)
    • 
          
  • Sidebar footer → Backup / Restore — full app data
    • 
        

      


      

        
Docs, Tabs & Zen

        
    
          
  •  new document
    • 
          
  • Click a doc or template's icon in the sidebar → pick from a small icon set (built-in templates excluded)
    • 
          
  •  close tab
    • 
          
  •  save now
    • 
          
  •  toggle zen mode
    • 
          
  • Esc exit zen mode only (doesn't toggle back in)
    • 
          
  •  Preview — read-only render; foldable, right-click for a laser pointer
    • 
          
  • Preview → ▶ Presenter mode — title slide, then one top-level section per slide, fullscreen · →/Space next · ← prev · Home/End first/last · G all slides · B blank screen · N notepad (same as this doc's Pad) · Q notes in Q&A view · Esc exit
    • 
          
  •  toggle toolbar
    • 
          
  •  toggle app bar — the reliable way back if you hide it, since Settings lives in that row too
    • 
        

      

      

        
Features

        
    
          
  • Settings →  Features — toggle optional capabilities on/off
    • 
          
  • A dot appears next to "Features" in the rail when any capability is off
    • 
          
  • Note Panel — per-node rich text notes
    • 
          
  • Pad Panel — one whole-document notepad per doc
    • 
          
  • AI Capabilities — Rewrite, Expand, Summarise, Outline, Tags, Icon, Restructure text
    • 
          
  • Decision Log — structured decision records + Excel export
    • 
          
  • Tagging — #tag chips on nodes
    • 
          
  • @ Mentions & Backlinks — link nodes; see incoming links in Note panel
    • 
          
  • Focus — zoom into a branch; Ctrl/Cmd + . to enter, Ctrl/Cmd + , to exit
    • 
          
  • Off = hidden, not deleted — data is always preserved; states saved across sessions
    • 
          
  •  hide tree lines — clean indented view, like Dynalist
    • 
        

      

      

        
Tabs

        
    
          
  • Click a doc in the sidebar or + at end of strip → open as tab
    • 
          
  •  new document
    • 
          
  •  close active tab (doc stays in sidebar)
    • 
          
  • Double-click tab → rename · Drag to reorder · ▾ → search open tabs
    • 
          
  • Each tab keeps its own undo history, scroll, and selection
    • 
          
  • Settings → Startup → "Reopen tabs on launch" restores them next session
    • 
        

      


      

        
Version History

        
    
          
  • Right-click a doc in the sidebar → "Version history" → automatic snapshots
    • 
          
  • Snapshot taken before content would be overwritten — at most once per ~10 min, capped at last 20 per document
    • 
          
  • "Save a version now" → snapshot immediately, useful before a big rewrite
    • 
          
  • Restoring saves current content as a version first — always reversible
    • 
        

      


      

        
Shortcuts

        
    
          
  • Settings → Keyboard Shortcuts → Customize — remap any action
    • 
          
  •  save now
    • 
          
  •  toggle toolbar
    • 
          
  •  Quick Assist — commands, or search anything
    • 
          
  •  generate outline (AI)
    • 
          
  •  restructure text into tree (AI)
    • 
          
  • Shortcuts blocked by OS/browser always have a matching button
    • 
        

      

      

      

        

      


    

    
Shortcuts blocked by the OS or browser always have a matching button or menu item that works instead.
